documents.text: treated with surgery alone, have long-term cognitive and adaptive-behavioral impairment (Ris et al . 2008 ). Improvements in pediatric low-grade glioma therapy have previously been slow to advance, and only recently, has the biology underlying these tumors begun to be molecularly character- ized, and potential therapeutic targets identiﬁ ed. Historically, glioma classiﬁ cation has relied on histological characterization. As, large-scale sequencing efforts utilizing whole-genome, tran- scriptome, and targeted high-throughput sequenc- ing of tumors have become available, recurrent genetic alterations driving aberrant cell signaling pathways have been identiﬁ ed. In 2008/2009, the identiﬁ cation of an oncogenic KIAA1549-BRAF fusion gene, resulting in constitutive BRAF activation was

documents.text: 2008/2009, the identiﬁ cation of an oncogenic KIAA1549-BRAF fusion gene, resulting in constitutive BRAF activation was described in the majority of pilo- cytic astrocytomas and ﬁ rst implicated BRAF in pediatric low-grade glioma development (Pﬁ ster et al . 2008 ; Sievert et al . 2009 ). Additional acti- vating mutations of BRAF, including BRAF V600E in WHO grade II pleiomorphic xanthoastrocyto- mas and diffuse astrocytomas (Zhang et al . 2013 ), new KIAA1549-BRAF fusion variants, and novel BRAF fusions and mutations in non-cerebellar pilocytic astrocytomas (Jones et al . 2013 ) further validate BRAF as an oncogenic driver in low- grade gliomas. More recently, recurrent muta- tions, fusions, and duplications affecting the tyrosine kinase domain in the ﬁ broblast growth factor receptor (

documents.text: 21 astrocytomas, which, like mutated BRAF , result in dysregulation of the mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK) and PI3K pathways (Fig. 2.3 ). The convergence of multiple, mutually exclusive genomic alterations in multiple pediatric low- grade glioma subtypes on a single, common sig- naling pathway (MAPK), conﬁ rm the importance of this signaling pathway in low-grade glioma development. Inhibitors of BRAF and of the MAPK pathway are currently in early phase clin- ical trials in pediatrics. Although these new tar- geted agents may be promising, they will likely need to be combined with traditional chemother- apy regimens or with other pathway inhibitors, as adult clinical trials reveal that drug resistance may develop rapidly with selective targeted inhibitors (Salama and Flaherty 2013

documents.text: trials reveal that drug resistance may develop rapidly with selective targeted inhibitors (Salama and Flaherty 2013 ). In addi- tion, it has been demonstrated that some RAF- speciﬁ c inhibitors paradoxically activate (instead of inhibit) the MAPK pathway (Sievert et al . 2013 ; Heidorn et al . 2010 ; Hatzivassiliou et al . 2010 ); deﬁ ning mechanisms of resistance is under investigation. Regardless, this creates an additional safety concern when conducting clini- cal trials in children with slow-growing tumors who may require treatment with these medica- tions for a prolonged period (months to years). Neuroﬁ bromatosis type 1 (NF-1) is caused by a germline, inherited or sporadic, inactivating mutation of the NF1 gene on chromosome 17, whose protein product neuroﬁ bromin negatively

Therefore, inactivation of neuroﬁ bromin causes increased activation of the RAS/RAF/ MEK/MAPK and the PI3K/AKT pathways (Fig. 2.3 ). SNP-microarray analysis of pediatric low-grade gliomas demon- strates that loss of NF1 and activating BRAF mutations are mutually exclusive (Pﬁ ster et al . 2008 ; Sievert et al . 2009 ; Jones et al. 2013 ), which further supports the importance of the MAPK pathway in glioma biology. The mTOR pathway, downstream of the PI3K pathway, also appears to play a role in low-grade glioma, espe- cially in the context of inherited predisposition syndromes. Children with Tuberous Sclerosis (TS) can have biallelic inactivation in either of the TS tumor suppressor genes (TSC1 or TSC2) that result in direct activation of mTOR pathway, and mTOR inhibition has demonstrated efﬁ cacy in treating TS-associated astrocytomas

documents.text: direct activation of mTOR pathway, and mTOR inhibition has demonstrated efﬁ cacy in treating TS-associated astrocytomas (Franz et al . 2013 ). In addition, up-regulation of mTOR has been noted in NF1-associated glioma mouse models (Banerjee et al . 2011 ). Inhibitors of mTOR are currently in clinical trials for children with TS and subependymal giant cell astrocy- toma and for children (with or without NF1) with recurrent or refractory low-grade glioma (Franz and Weiss 2012 ). Future therapies for pediatric low-grade glio- mas are currently under development with bio- logic rationale. Collaborative basic and translational research efforts are identifying the molecular characteristics and biochemical signa- tures for this diverse set of tumors. documents.text: characteristics and biochemical signa- tures for this diverse set of tumors. Targeted agents carry the potential of improved tumor con- trol with limited toxicity; however, the burden of proving their effectiveness in a low-grade tumor will be much higher as the overall survival for these children is more favorable than those with high-grade tumors. Goals of therapy should focus not only on improving progression free survival but on minimizing the morbidity to long-term survivors. documents.text: The prognosis is still extremely poor despite advances in the cancer therapy. The growth and spread of gliomas within the host tissue depends upon their abil- ity to stimulate new vessel formation, the angiogenesis, to get nutrients and remove waste products. Therefore, blocking the action of angiogenic factors, or utilizing angiogenic suppressors to inhibit angiogenesis, constitute a new strategy to suppress tumor growth. A representative drug is Avastin (Bevacizumab). It has been proved by FDA to treat patients with recurrent glioblastoma. It is a protein that acts against vessel growth factor. However, there are many vessel growth factors and Avastin only target one of them. Therefore the inhibition effect for vessel growth can be com- pensated by other angiogenic factors. Other

documents.text: one of them. Therefore the inhibition effect for vessel growth can be com- pensated by other angiogenic factors. Other disadvantages such as drug resistance and increased vascular cooption of the host vascu- lature have been reported. Kringle 1-5 (K1-5), discovered by Cao, is also a protein aimed on the vessels. Compared with Avastin, K1-5 acts directly on endothelial cells and leads endo- thelial cell death. documents.text: 26 of K1-5 was proven in a murine hepatocellular carcinoma model. In this chapter the therapeu- tic effect of K1-5 in malignant gliomas was discussed. We demonstrated that K1-5 is effective in the treatment of malignant glio- mas. It is effective not only in the inhibition of vascular growth but also in limiting tumor invasiveness and improving tumor hypoxia, and then lead to a longer survival. Introduction Antiangiogenic therapy has been a new choice for cancer therapy during the past decade. The theory is based on the angiogenesis-dependent tumor growth. Therefore, suppressing angiogenesis may decrease tumor growth. Among various proangiogenic fac- tors, vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) is a major angiogenic factor. Agents target VEGF sig- naling axis, such as VEGF neutralizing

documents.text: growth factor (VEGF) is a major angiogenic factor. Agents target VEGF sig- naling axis, such as VEGF neutralizing antibody and agents that acts on VEGF receptor, has become a main interest for many cancer treatments. These drugs demonstrate some improvement in certain type of tumors, however, the overall beneﬁ t was modest. The most promising antiangiogenic drug currently available for treating recurrent glioblas- toma, one of the most frustrating diseases, is bevaci- zumab. However, there are still some controversies regarding using bevacizumab for this brain tumor. Besides VEGF axis, another target is endothelial cells because they are relative stable targets compar- ing to tumor cells, in either genetically (mutation) or drug resistance aspect. K1-5, discovered by Cao et al. ( 1999 ),

documents.text: to tumor cells, in either genetically (mutation) or drug resistance aspect. K1-5, discovered by Cao et al. ( 1999 ), is currently the most potent endoge- nous angiogenesis inhibitor in the laboratory. It is so far the most potent antiangiogenic agent in the krin- gle family in inhibiting endothelial proliferation, migration, and tumor growth (in animal models). However, studies on K1-5 are still limited. Therefore we were interested in its effect in treating malignant gliomas. We implanted C6 glioma cells into the rat brains. K1-5 was administrated via carotid artery 5 days later. The promising treatment results in this rat glioma model were published in Neurosurgery in 2012 (Lin et al. 2012 ). In this chapter, we dis- cussed what we learn from K1-5 in a rat glioma model and highlight the

documents.text: (Lin et al. 2012 ). In this chapter, we dis- cussed what we learn from K1-5 in a rat glioma model and highlight the main difference between K1-5 and anti-VEGF therapy, especially in its abil- ity to reduce tumor invasion and hypoxia. Angiogenesis Angionenesis is a process for the formation of new vessels. It includes proliferation of endothelial cells and alignment of these cells to form a tube struc- tures (vasculogenesis), elongation and branching of the newly formed vessels into the tissues (angio- genesis), and the differentiation of these vessels into arteries (arteriogenesis). The process of angio- genesis is controlled by positive proangiogenetic factors as well as negative anti- angiogenetic fac- tors. The proangiogenetic factors include VEGF, ﬁ broblast growth factor (FGF),

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: as negative anti- angiogenetic fac- tors. The proangiogenetic factors include VEGF, ﬁ broblast growth factor (FGF), epidermal growth factor (EGF), endostatin, tumstatin, canstatin, etc. Normally, these two effects are balanced to main- tain the stability of vascular structure. Angiogenesis is of key importance in keeping the growth and spread of malignant tumors. Tumor cells can secrete proangiogenic factors to stimulate the formation of new blood vessels. They can also stimulate surrounding cells to secrete these factors to reach the same goal. For example, a glioma cell can secrete VEGF into surrounding tissues. These VEGF can bind to the VEGF receptor in the endothelial cell mem- branes. After a series of relay proteins, these sig- nals reach the cell nucleus to stimulate cell mitosis

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: cell mem- branes. After a series of relay proteins, these sig- nals reach the cell nucleus to stimulate cell mitosis as well as activation of certain genes for the production of even more VEGF. These newly formed vessels can in one hand feed the tumors with nutrient and oxygen, and on the other hand to allow tumors cells to invade nearby tissues as well as to metastasize distantly. Therefore, block- ing angiogenesis can theoretically inhibit the growth and migration of malignant tumors. Importance of Angiogenesis in Gliomas Malignant glioma is one of the most commonly seen malignant central nervous system tumors. In gliomas, angiogenesis plays a crucial role in its growth and the development. The growth and M.-C. Huang and Y.-L. Lin

documents.text: 27 spread of gliomas within the host tissue depends upon their ability to obtain nutrients and remove waste products. Malignant gliomas initiate angio- genesis and neovasculation by disrupting the nor- mal homeostasis between angiogenic and antiangiogenic factors. The level of angiogenesis is correlated with the aggressiveness of gliomas and is a useful marker for patient’s prognosis (Birlik et al. 2006 ). Structurally, the tumor vessels in gliomas are dilated, tortuous, and disorganized (Fig. 3.1a ). The rapid proliferation of endothelial cell causes variable thickness of vessel walls with multiple layers of endothelial cells to obliterate the vascu- lar lumen. Therefore, although there is dense vas- cular structure in malignant gliomas, the actual tissue perfusion is poor leading to

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)

documents.text: although there is dense vas- cular structure in malignant gliomas, the actual tissue perfusion is poor leading to tumor necrosis. In fact, multiple layers of endothelial cells and tumor necrosis are the characteristic pathological features of a glioblastoma (Jain et al. 2007 ). Furthermore, the leakage of tumor vasculature and the recruitment of pericytes result in the impairment of the blood-brain barrier (BBB) and then cause vasogenic oedema and increase of interstitial ﬂ uid pressure. In general, long-term corticosteroid treatment is required to control vasogenic edema. However, numerous side effects may accompany chronic corticosteroid usage. documents.text: Tumor Origin In Dec. 2010, two research groups identiﬁ ed that some blood vessels in tumors are originated from tumor cells. Wang et al. ( 2010 ) demonstrated that stem-like cells (CD133) without endothelial lineage characters (CD144 vascular endothelia- cadherin, maker for endothelial progenitors) are capable of differentiating into endothelial progenitor cells while retaining its tumor stem cell characters at the same time (CD133 + /CD144 + ) when promoted by extrinsic signals such as co- culturing with tumor- derived endothelial cells. A subset of CD133 + / CD144 + cell is also observed in the collected human glioblastoma specimens. Although this subset is a very small fraction (1.4%), it may provoke the pro- gression of glioblastoma. Ricci-Vitiani et al. ( 2010 ) analyzed endothelial

( 2010 ) analyzed endothelial cells from glioblastoma with remarkable angiogenesis, 20–90% of these endo- thelial cells show glioblastoma-speciﬁ c chromo- somal aberration, which may indicate that these endothelial cells have tumor origin. Antiangiogenesis in Treating Malignant Gliomas The prognosis of malignant gliomas is extremely poor despite extensive efforts at surgery (such as adding intraoperative neuromonitoring and 5-aminolevulinic acid (5-ALA) to increase tumor resection), radiotherapy (such as adding volumetric- modulated radiation treatment tech- nique (RapidArc), an intensity modulated radia- tion therapy (IMRT) technique and a 3D conformal technique), chemotherapy (such as adding intraop-

documents.text: modulated radia- tion therapy (IMRT) technique and a 3D conformal technique), chemotherapy (such as adding intraop- erative Gliadel and postoperative temozolamide). One of the reasons for its poor prognosis is that these tumors are “smart” tumor which can obtain resistance to the therapeutic agents and these sur- vived tumor cells (most likely cancer stem cells) may become more malignant and regrowth (recur- rent) (Chen et al. 2012 ). Therefore, the future ther- apy for malignant gliomas relies on ﬁ nding new targets or new combination of treatment. Among many targets that are currently under investigation, antiangionenesis has gained the most attention because malignant gliomas is highly vascularized. Since 2000s, a wide range of antiangiogenic factors has been approved by FDA for

documents.text: gliomas is highly vascularized. Since 2000s, a wide range of antiangiogenic factors has been approved by FDA for clinical use and clinical trials for cancer therapy. Glioblastoma has been in trials of some drugs approved for other indications. This included: Erlotnib (phase II, approved for lung cancer in China, 2004), Sorafenib (phase II, approved for kidney cancer in the US, 2005), and lenalidomide (phase II, approved for myelodys- plastic syndrome in the US, 2005). Currently, bevaciumab was the only antian- giogenic drug proved by FDA to use in recurrent glioblastoma. Bevaciumab is a neutralizing anti- body for VEGF. In two single-arm trials, bevaci- umab was proven to be effective in treating recurrent glioblastoma with a median response duration of 4.2 and 3.9 months, respectively.

documents.text: 29 in May 2009. Bevaciumab has also been showed to be effective in prolonging median survival when combing with chemotherapy or radiotherapy (8.2–9.7 and 12.5 months, respectively). It is effective at delaying the progression of brain tumors, too. Beside its antiangiogenetic effect, a recent study by von Baumgarten et al. ( 2011 ) has demonstrated a potential indirect antimumoral effect in high dose of bevacizumab. However, there are some concerns. Similar to chemotherapy and all other targeted therapies, malignant gliomas develop resistance to anti- VEGF signaling axis. During the past few years, growing clinical evidences indicate that the ther- apeutic effects of antiangiogenic drugs are not as promising as expected. The most signiﬁ cant improvement is the reduction of brain edema,

documents.text: drugs are not as promising as expected. The most signiﬁ cant improvement is the reduction of brain edema, according to MRI T2 image (Pope et al. 2006 ). It is likely, at least partly, due to the inhibition of VEGF effect in the progression of brain edema (Lucio-Eterovic et al. 2009 ), since VEGF, in addition to its antiangiogenic activity, can increase the vessel permeability and thus lead to cerebral edema. However, there are other agents that can reduce brain edema in a much lower cost. It is thus doubtful to use such an expensive drug in the cost effectiveness perspective. In addition to brain tumor, the use of Avastin in other cancers is also disappointing. In 2010, FDA revoked the approval of the breast cancer indica- tion for Avastin because it is not beneﬁ cial for life quality and

Beside anti-VEGF axis, clinical tri- als targeting other angiogenesis agents such as epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) or platelet-derived growth factor receptor (PDGFR), which are also overexpressed in glioblastomas, were not promising (Stupp et al. 2006 ). However, less attention was paid to endogenous angiogenesis inhibitors despite that many of them have been discovered for more than a decade. Kringle 1-5 (K1-5), discovered by Cao et al. ( 1999 ), is currently the most potent endogenous angiogenesis inhibitor in the laboratory. The Structure and Antiangenetic Effect of the Kringle In 1994, O’Reilly et al. ( 1994 ) isolated a 38 kDa protein from a subclone of Lewis lung carcinoma and named it angiostatin. It is a circulating angio- genesis inhibitor in serum and urine of mice bear- ing a murine Lewis lung carcinoma. Amino acid sequence analysis revealed that angiostatin was

documents.text: and urine of mice bear- ing a murine Lewis lung carcinoma. Amino acid sequence analysis revealed that angiostatin was identical to an internal fragment of plasminogen. This is a potent inhibitor of angiogenesis which showed speciﬁ cally inhibition to endothelial cells, not to other cell type. The mechanism and their signaling events remain unclear. Proteolytic fragments of human plasminogen are similar to the murine angiostatin and both showed strong inhibition on the proliferation of capillary endo- thelial cells. Structurally, plasminogen contains ﬁ ve disulﬁ de-linked structures, known as kringle domains, in total; angiostatin contains the ﬁ rst three or four kringle domains (K1-3, or K1-4). Later on, Cao et al. ( 1996 ) made recombinant fragments of multiple and individual kringle

documents.text: domains (K1-3, or K1-4). Later on, Cao et al. ( 1996 ) made recombinant fragments of multiple and individual kringle domains and compared their inhibition effect on endothelial cell proliferation. The function of kringle family is different from its intact parental molecule, plasminogen, in inhibiting endothelial proliferation. This inhibition effect of K1 was greater K4, and K5 was greater than angiostatin. In 1999, Cao et al. ( 1999 ) further made recombi- nant K1-5. Signiﬁ cant inhibition of the vascular tree by K1-5 was proved in the chick chorio- allantoic membrane, the mouse corneal micro- pocket, and murine ﬁ brosarcoma. Structurally, K1-5 contains angiostatin (K1-4) and most of the K5 domain; functionally, it has the same potency in the inhibition of endothelial cell proliferation

One mechanism of K1-5 is its ability to bind to endothelial ATP synthase, which activates the caspase-apoptotic pathway and results in endothelial cell apoptosis (Veitonmaki et al. 2004 ). The antiangiogenic abil- ity in tumor was also reported in murine hepato- cellular carcinomas (Schmitz et al. 2007 ) and human breast tumors in nude mice (Galaup et al. 2005 ). In glioma models, angiostatin and K5 have been shown to have an effect on inhibiting angio- genesis and migration (Kirsch et al. 1998 ; Perri et al. 2005 ). We also demonstrated that K1-5 exerted promising results in the rat glioma model with inhibition of tumor growth, tumor spreading, and

Discussion K1-5 Inhibits Tumor Angiogenesis Abnormal vascularization is a hallmark of glio- mas, functionally and structurally. Morphologically, tumor blood vessels are dilated and tortuous, which may lead to poor efﬁ ciency of supplying oxygen and nutrients. Moreover, the uneven vessel lumen may obstruct drug delivery, which decreases therapeutic efﬁ cacy. In addition to the excessive proliferation of endothelial cells, pericytes and basement membranes are also abnormal. These leaky vessels result in the increase of interstitial ﬂ uid pressure. In the K1-5 treated tumor, the ves- sels were straighter, and the vessels length and

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: of interstitial ﬂ uid pressure. In the K1-5 treated tumor, the ves- sels were straighter, and the vessels length and density were also decreased signiﬁ cantly. The dis- tribution of vessel size shifted to smaller diameter (<20 μm: ~60%). In contrast, the majority of C6 gliomas had vessel diameter in 20~80 μm. Therefore, K1-5 is potent in trimming and normal- izes tumor vessels in our model (Fig. 3.1b ). K1-5 Decreases Hypoxic Area of the Glioma Another concern of antiangiogenic agents is that severely destroyed vasculature may compromise oxygen delivery which leads to tumor hypoxia, which in turn can lead to an increase in tumor invasion (de Groot and Milano 2009 ). The aggressiveness of malignant brain tumor has been associated with tumor hypoxia (Evans et al. 2004 ). Tumor hypoxia leads

documents.text: of malignant brain tumor has been associated with tumor hypoxia (Evans et al. 2004 ). Tumor hypoxia leads to resistance of tumor treatment, the induction of genetic insta- bility, the progression of aggressive tumor phe- notypes (Reynolds et al. 1996 ; Harris 2002 ), the migration and invasion of glioma cells (Gessler et al. 2010 ), the recruitment of bone marrow- derived vascular modulatory cells (Du et al. 2008 ), and survival of glioblastoma stem cells (Heddleston et al. 2009 ). In K1-5 treatment tumor, we observed signiﬁ cant reduction of hypoxic area and HIF-1 α expression, which causes less pseudopalisading necrosis in the treated animals (Fig. 3.1c, d ). Antibodies against VEGF or VEGFR-2 were also effective at inhibit- ing tumor and vessel growth; however, these agents promote a

documents.text: against VEGF or VEGFR-2 were also effective at inhibit- ing tumor and vessel growth; however, these agents promote a strong tumor invasion and hypoxia (Lamszus et al. 2005 ; de Groot and Milano 2009 ). Since K1-5 directly acts on endothelial cells by binding to endothelial ATP synthase which triggers the caspase-apoptotic pathway, it is likely that the proliferating endothe- lial cells are killed and non-proliferating endothe- lial cells (or “stable” vessels) remain. Therefore, it is reasonable to speculate that the remaining nor- malized vessels could improve tissue perfusion thus limit the devastating effect of hypoxia. Lower VEGF Expression in K1-5 Treated Glioma Another characteristic of glioma tissues is a high level of VEGF which is mainly responsible by tumor hypoxia. High level of

documents.text: characteristic of glioma tissues is a high level of VEGF which is mainly responsible by tumor hypoxia. High level of VEGF has been asso- ciated with high-grade malignancy (Chaudhry et al. 2001 ) and is a poor prognostic factor for patients (Jain et al. 2007 ). Furthermore, VEGF can lead to vasogenic edema, which also relate to a poor prog- nosis. Currently, bevacizumab is the most promi- nent drugs targeting VEGF. Unlike bevacizumab that targets VEGF directly, no evidence showed that K1-5 act on VEGF ligands or receptors. Instead, the lower VEGF expression in the K1-5 treated group may be contributed by the improvement of tumor hypoxia. The decreased production of HIF M.-C. Huang and Y.-L. Lin

Inhibition Effect of K1-5 on Glioma Cell Invasion One of the characters of malignant gliomas is dif- fuse parenchymal inﬁ ltration. It grows fast and inﬁ l- trate aggressively into neighboring areas of the brain tissue, which destroy neurological function, lead to poor survival, and worsen the quality of life. The ill-deﬁ ned tumor area complicates thorough surgical resection. Therefore, the ﬁ nding of lower degree of tumor cell invasion in the K1-5 treated group is noteworthy (Fig. 3.1e, f ). As one of the main concerns of bevacizumb is that it increases the diffuse inﬁ ltration of glioma (Keunen et al. 2011

In contrast, K1-5 may restrain the tumor cell inﬁ ltra- tion by obliterating tumor vascular growth. Because tumor cells favor to migrate along vessels and pro- liferate at or near vascular branches (Farin et al. 2006 ), K1-5 might impede tumor proliferation and perivascular inﬁ ltration by abolishing angiogenesis and pruning the hypervascularity. However, we have to point out that human malignant gliomas not only inﬁ ltrate the adjacent tissue along the vessels, they also inﬁ

documents.text: have to point out that human malignant gliomas not only inﬁ ltrate the adjacent tissue along the vessels, they also inﬁ ltrate extensively along white matter tracts. We do not know if K1-5 inhibits those vessel- independent inﬁ ltrating glioma cells. Recently, Zhang et al. ( 2012 ) demonstrated the combination of bevacizumab with angiostatin appears to decrease the bevacizumab-induced invasion in a glioma model. This may provide new insight of combina- tion treatment. Furthermore, it has been documented that angiogenesis and inﬁ ltration of human gliomas may be associated with the function of macro- phages (Nishie et al. 1999 ). Human angiostatin (K1-3) has been shown to inhibit monocyte/mac- rophage migration (Perri et al. 2007b ). Besides endothelial cell and macrophages, angiostatin

documents.text: to inhibit monocyte/mac- rophage migration (Perri et al. 2007b ). Besides endothelial cell and macrophages, angiostatin also inhibits the migration of cancer cells (Perri et al. 2007a ). In a glioma model, Perri et al. ( 2005 ) dem- onstrated that the anti-tumorigenic effect of K5 relied not only on inhibiting endothelial cells but also on suppressing the recruitment of tumor-inﬁ l- trating macrophages. Therefore, reduction of mac- rophages might contribute to the inhibition of tumor-associated macrophage angiogenesis. Although we don’t have direct evidence, it is spec- ulated that K1-5 may possess similar function as angiostatin and K5. Further studies characterizing this function are warranted. K1-5 Treated Animals Had Longer Survival The application of K1-5 resulted in a signiﬁ cant decrease in tumor volumes and a longer survival time (Fig. 3.2 ). Recently Jain’s lab demonstrated that vascular normalization can increase tumor per- fusion and improve survival in patients who responds to anti-VEGF treatment (Sorensen et al. 2012 ). Because K1-5 is potent in normalizing tumor vessel, longer survival may be achieved. Further studies for longer treatment period are needed. Conclusion In Conclusion our result has shown that K1-5 is a potential drug for treating malignant gliomas. In spite of its direct antiangiogenetic effect by Fig. 3.2 K1-5 prolongs the survival. By decreasing tumor hypoxia, it can decrease tumor growth, tumor invasion, and VEGF production. The decrease of VEGF pro- duction itself may have the beneﬁ cial effect such as decreasing cerebral edema to improve neuro- logical status and patient survival (Fig. 3.3 ). However, the effect of K1-5 is still in its prelimi- nary experimental status. More studies on differ- ent animal models and its exact mechanism are warranted before jumping into conclusions. Three parallel/coexisting mechanisms involved in the processes according to the angiogenesis stage. The endothelial pathway: the action of K1-5 on endo- thelial cells results in cell apoptosis and decreasing vessel numbers. The lack of nutrition eventually starves tumor cells. The second pathway is by the action of anti- neovascularization. The inhibition of vessel branching and migration decreases the tumor invasion at the same time, which reduces the tumor growth. documents.text: in Meningiomas After 5-ALA Pretreatment 37 Photodynamic Therapy (PDT) in Meningiomas 38 References 40 Abstract Meningiomas represent 30% of all intracranial neoplasms. They are predominantly slow growing extra axial brain tumours arising from arachnoidal cells and usually have an excellent prognosis if completely removed. However, radical tumour removal near vital functional structures and areas of inﬁ ltration is not always possible and histologically “benign” meningiomas may exhibit aggres- sive behaviour like inﬁ ltration and early recur- rence. Biological markers that would make areas of inﬁ ltration visible by ﬂ uorescence might therefore have a signiﬁ cant impact on patient survival and quality of life. 5-Aminolevulinic acid (5-ALA) is a pre- cursor in the cellular heme

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: impact on patient survival and quality of life. 5-Aminolevulinic acid (5-ALA) is a pre- cursor in the cellular heme biosynthesis. The application of exogenous 5-ALA leads to an intracellular accumulation of protoporphyrin IX (PpIX), causing these PpIX saturated cells to become ﬂ uorescent and photosensitive under light of an appropriate wavelength. 5-ALA induced PpIX ﬂ uorescence has the ability to deﬁ ne inﬁ ltration zones into dural structures and bone and mark residual menin- gioma tissue. It has the potential to facilitate meningioma resection and to individualize the extent of dural resection for each patient in order to prevent morbidity. Speciﬁ c intracellular accumulation of PpIX can be used for photodynamic therapy (PDT). Induction of selective apoptosis, reduction of

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)

documents.text: 36 tumour vessel density and no risk of secondary carcinogenesis make PDT an ideal treatment option for meningiomas. Due to the variable potentials for PpIX accumulation within dif- ferent meningioma subtypes, further research is required to ensure sufﬁ ciently intense ﬂ uo- rescence to enable PDT in these cases. Introduction Meningiomas are predominantly slow growing extra axial brain tumours arising from arachnoi- dal cells. They have a female predominance of middle to late adult life and make up nearly 30% of all intracranial neoplasms (Louis et al. 2007 ). Ninety percent of these lesions are of WHO grade I and have an excellent prognosis once the tumour is completely removed (Simpson 1957 ). The importance of radical resection on the outcome of patients with meningiomas regard- less of

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: (Simpson 1957 ). The importance of radical resection on the outcome of patients with meningiomas regard- less of histological grade is well established. Non-radical resections show a 4.2-fold higher mortality during the second to the 15th postop- erative year as compared to patients in whom the tumour was completely removed (Kallio et al. 1992 ). However, complete tumour removal is often not achieved since inﬁ ltration into bone and dura is hard to judge intraoperatively and complex topographical arrangements of vital functional structures frequently make surgery challenging (Hashemi et al. 2010 ). Accordingly, recurrent tumour is sometimes found at multiple sites, indi- cating tumour remnants left behind during a pre- vious resection (Philippon and Cornu 1991 ). Not only atypical and

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: cating tumour remnants left behind during a pre- vious resection (Philippon and Cornu 1991 ). Not only atypical and anaplastic meningio- mas, but also “benign” grade I meningiomas may present with an aggressive behaviour such as penetration of the arachnoid membrane, destruc- tion of bone and regrowth of residual tumour. This speciﬁ c aggressive “subtype” within grade I meningiomas is indistinguishable in vivo from the more benign grade I meningioma (Pﬁ sterer et al. 2007 ) and the inﬁ ltrating portion is easily missed in all types as demonstrated by the up to 20% recurrence rate of these tumours. The extent of resection according to the Simpson grading system (Simpson 1957 ) is cur- rently the standard by which surgical quality is established and continues to be used as a basis for

documents.text: 1957 ) is cur- rently the standard by which surgical quality is established and continues to be used as a basis for clinical outcome. Small tumour remnants may be impossible to identify on MRI scans, and dural inﬁ ltration, bone invasion and arachnoid border destruction can be missed even with intraopera- tive imaging and navigation techniques. Biological markers that would make these areas of inﬁ ltration visible by ﬂ uorescence might there- fore have a signiﬁ cant impact on patient survival and quality of life. Photodynamic Diagnosis (PDD) in Meningiomas Several case reports as well as small illustrative series where ﬂ uorescence was used for resection guidance in meningioma patients have been pub- lished. The reports were encouraging: a strong, sometimes homogenous ﬂ uorescence marked

documents.text: patients have been pub- lished. The reports were encouraging: a strong, sometimes homogenous ﬂ uorescence marked the tumour in most cases, regardless of histopatho- logical subtype, tumour grading or proliferation indices (Kajimoto et al. 2007 ). Visualizing the main tumour bulk in menin- giomas can usually be achieved macroscopi- cally, or with the use of an operating microscope, without adjuvant visualisation tools such as ﬂ uorescence. Without a pressing need to more speciﬁ cally mark neoplastic tissue, ﬂ uores- cence guided resection in meningiomas was hardly ever published. Using 20 mg/kg body weight of 5-aminolevulinic acid (5-ALA) 4 h prior to surgery to induce protoporphyrin IX (PpIX) accumulation in their meningioma patients, Kajimoto, Morofuji and others dem- onstrated positive

documents.text: IX (PpIX) accumulation in their meningioma patients, Kajimoto, Morofuji and others dem- onstrated positive ﬂ uorescence not only within the main tumour bulk, but also within areas of meningeal tumour inﬁ ltration and areas of bone invasion, regardless of tumour grading (Kajimoto et al. 2007 ; Morofuji et al. 2008 ). In accordance with the well known ﬁ nding that dura inﬁ ltration shown in enhanced MRI and CT scans as “dural tail” mostly represents venous congestion (Kawahara et al. 2001 ), they M. Hefti and G. von Campe

documents.text: 37 could elegantly demonstrate that within the areas of “hypertrophic dura” only limited ﬂ uo- rescence is encountered, and that these regions of ﬂ uorescence could be clearly distinguished from non-tumoral hypertrophic dura (Kajimoto et al. 2007 ). More interesting still was the fact that corre- sponding areas of tumour invasion into bony structures of the skull showed positive ﬂ uores- cence, visible through a specially ﬁ tted operat- ing microscope. Although in most cases of meningiomas hyperostosis is due to tumour inﬁ ltration (Pieper et al. 1999 ), invasion of meningioma cells into bony structures has also been demonstrated in patients without radio- graphically identiﬁ ed hyperostosis (Pieper et al. 1999 ). The extent of bone involvement in meningioma patients is therefore

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: identiﬁ ed hyperostosis (Pieper et al. 1999 ). The extent of bone involvement in meningioma patients is therefore impossible to delineate reliably with neuronavigation or intra- operative imaging. Moreover intraoperative pathological diagnosis cannot be accurately per- formed, since bone specimens need demineral- ization prior to histological examination. Bone invasion however directly affects outcome in meningioma patients (Gabeau- Lacet et al. 2009 ): an accurate visualization of tumour inﬁ l- tration within these hyperostotic areas would therefore facilitate complete resection and avoid unnecessary openings of nasal sinuses and pneumatised bone at the skull base, thus directly impacting outcome. Tumour speciﬁ c biomark- ers, such as 5-ALA induced PpIX ﬂ uorescence, could therefore

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: thus directly impacting outcome. Tumour speciﬁ c biomark- ers, such as 5-ALA induced PpIX ﬂ uorescence, could therefore provide a new visualisation modality, as shown by preliminary data and quantitative ﬂ uorescence analysis of bone inva- sion within selected patients (Morofuji et al. 2008 ) (Kajimoto et al. 2007 ). 5-Aminolevulinic Acid 5-Aminolevulinic acid (5-ALA) is a precursor in the cellular heme biosynthesis, regulated by a negative feedback mechanism through the con- trol of ALA synthetase by free heme. The nega- tive feedback mechanism can be overcome by providing the cells with an excess amount of exogenous 5-ALA, resulting in intracellular accumulation of ﬂ uorescent porphyrins – mainly PpIX –, which causes the cells to become ﬂ uorescent and photosensitive. Cellular uptake of

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: porphyrins – mainly PpIX –, which causes the cells to become ﬂ uorescent and photosensitive. Cellular uptake of 5-ALA, increased PpIX synthesis, reduced PpIX conversion into heme and low availability of iron are probably the most relevant factors affecting the potential amount of PpIX accumulation within a cell. Available data show that even though all these factors play a role in the heme synthesis in menin- gioma cells, the highest impact on PpIX accumu- lation stems from the lowered conversion of PpIX into heme by the reduced activity of the enzyme ferrochelatase (FCH) (Hefti et al. 2011 ). Intratumoral Heterogeneity and Fluorescence Intensity in Meningiomas After 5-ALA Pretreatment Differences in ﬂ uorescence intensity after 5-ALA application cannot only be seen between different

documents.text: 5-ALA Pretreatment Differences in ﬂ uorescence intensity after 5-ALA application cannot only be seen between different meningiomas (Bekelis et al. 2011 ), but also on an intra-tumoral level (Hefti 2011 ). Even histologi- cally conﬁ rmed tumour tissue can show no visi- ble level of ﬂ uorescence, but still accumulate signiﬁ cant PpIX amounts to allow a distinction from the surrounding normal tissue when a more sensitive method than the human eye is used for detection (Bekelis et al. 2011 ). This phenomenon has already been extensively described in high grade gliomas, where it is mostly due to inﬁ ltra- tion, necrosis, and areas of lower malignancy (Hefti et al. 2008 ). The exact mechanisms for the variable PpIX accumulation between meningi- oma cells within the same tumour are however not

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)

documents.text: exact mechanisms for the variable PpIX accumulation between meningi- oma cells within the same tumour are however not yet understood. Differences in FCH activity and synthesis were shown to be the main reason for varying concentrations of 5-ALA induced PpIX accumu- lation between two different benign meningioma cells lines (Hefti et al. 2011 ). Yet, there may be multiple other reasons for these differences out- side of the actual tumour biology, including tumour vascularity and hemodynamics. 4 Fluorescence Guided Resection and Photodynamic Therapy in Meningiomas

documents.text: 38 In terms of tumour biology, meningiomas are genetically heterogeneous entities that display different patterns of chromosomal changes, with the presence of more than one tumour cell clone in almost half of the cases (Sayagues et al. 2004 ). These molecular changes may precede histopath- ological changes and therefore have clinical implications in as far as removal of the more malignant areas of a heterogeneous tumour has signiﬁ cant impact on patient survival (Scheck et al. 1996 ). The question whether heterogeneity in ﬂ uo- rescence intensity and molecular heterogeneity correlate to some degree is crucial in deﬁ ning the usefulness of 5-ALA induced ﬂ uorescence for resection control in meningiomas (Whitson et al. 2011 ). For, if 5-ALA induced PpIX ﬂ uorescence would not only show inﬁ

documents.text: control in meningiomas (Whitson et al. 2011 ). For, if 5-ALA induced PpIX ﬂ uorescence would not only show inﬁ ltration reliably (Fig. 4.1 ), but intensity would also coincide with more malignant tumour areas (with extremely low or no ﬂ uorescence in benign areas), 5-ALA induced ﬂ uorescence would be a promising tool for resection guidance, indeed. Photodynamic Therapy (PDT) in Meningiomas Although Simpson Grade I and II resections can be achieved with microsurgical techniques in deep seated skull base lesions, sometimes com- plemented by endoscopy and wide bone removal, extensive surgery in these areas often comes at a price of new neurological deﬁ cits. Eloquent ana- tomical structures, the brainstem, cranial nerves (to which the tumour is often adherent), blood vessels or dural sinuses

documents.text: structures, the brainstem, cranial nerves (to which the tumour is often adherent), blood vessels or dural sinuses are regularly compro- mised (Hashemi et al. 2010 ). Alternative strate- gies are therefore used to achieve local tumour control after intentional subtotal resection, mostly in the form of radiation therapy either as stereotactic radiosurgery or using radionucleo- tides (Bartolomei et al. 2009 ). While generally yielding satisfactory to good results, high-energy irradiation has the potential of causing extensive DNA damage within the targeted cells, triggering mutations and carcinogenesis. documents.text: 39 rate, that potential may eventually lead to tumour induction in itself. Photodynamic therapy (PDT) consists of the administration and tumour uptake of a photo- sensitizer, followed by irradiation with light of an appropriate wavelength. PDT has the poten- tial to produce selective necrosis and apoptosis of tumour cells without causing damage to the surrounding normal tissue (Dougherty 1993 ). It thus offers a very selective treatment modality, and consequently seems ideal for brain tumours that often inﬁ ltrate normal brain and/or are seated in close vicinity to eloquent areas. As photosensitizers tend not to accumulate in cell nuclei, but at the inner mitochondrial wall in the case of 5-ALA mediated PpIX accumulation, PDT has a low potential for causing DNA dam- age, chromosomal

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: wall in the case of 5-ALA mediated PpIX accumulation, PDT has a low potential for causing DNA dam- age, chromosomal mutations or carcinogenesis. Experimental and clinical studies demonstrated that PDT complements the existing traditional tumour therapies like surgical resection, radia- tion therapy and chemotherapy (Schmidt et al. 2004 ). Compared to other adjuvant therapies, resistance to PDT has not been encountered (Fisher et al. 1995 ). Additionally, the effect of PDT is not only related to apoptosis and/or necrosis, but also to tumour vessel damage lead- ing to secondary cell death (Fisher et al. 1995). PDT could reduce the necessity for aggressive and potential hazardous surgery in the vicinity of vital structures and carries no risk of second- ary carcinogenesis in a disease with a

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: surgery in the vicinity of vital structures and carries no risk of second- ary carcinogenesis in a disease with a relevant life expectancy. It does not interfere with estab- lished therapies, and targets tumour vascular structures. These characteristics would make meningiomas an ideal candidate for PDT. Early preclinical studies on PDT in brain tumours begun in 1972 with glioma cell cultures (Diamond et al. 1972 ); subsequent clinical stud- ies on PDT in brain tumours were published in growing numbers over the last three decades. Clinical as well as experimental studies almost exclusively involve malignant gliomas and their recurrences with the odd exception of solitary anaplastic meningioma cases published as illus- trative reports (Kostron et al. 1988 ; Stylli and Kaye 2006 ). Although

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: anaplastic meningioma cases published as illus- trative reports (Kostron et al. 1988 ; Stylli and Kaye 2006 ). Although experimental studies demonstrated PDT efﬁ cacy on benign as well as on malignant primary meningioma cell cultures (Marks et al. 1992 ; Malham et al. 1996 ), enthusiasm for PDT in a less “dismal” brain tumour than high grade gliomas remained low. This is partly due to the speciﬁ c side effects of PDT, like increased intracranial pressure, oedema and prolonged skin photosensitization. These side effects depend on the type of photosensi- tizer, its concentration, the duration of light expo- sure and the light intensity used (e.g.: heamatoporphyrin derivates (HPD), meta- tetrahydroxyphenylchlorin (mTHPC, Foscan)) (Kostron 2010 ). 5-ALA has the advantage of an improved

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: (HPD), meta- tetrahydroxyphenylchlorin (mTHPC, Foscan)) (Kostron 2010 ). 5-ALA has the advantage of an improved “tumour to normal cell” uptake ratio and a skin photosensitization duration of only a few hours, as compared to other exogenous porphyrins. This translates into less or no damage to surrounding structures and signiﬁ cantly less brain oedema (Lilge and Wilson 1998 ), making it an ideally suited photosensitizer for intracranial lesions. Insufﬁ cient 5-ALA induced PpIX accumula- tion in major tissues of mesoderm origin was demonstrated in 1992 (Kennedy and Pottier 1992 ). Meningiomas are neoplasms derived from the arachnoidal cap cells and thus considered by most of mesoderm origin. Consistent with these ﬁ ndings, later in vitro studies showed inadequate concentrations of PpIX in

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: of mesoderm origin. Consistent with these ﬁ ndings, later in vitro studies showed inadequate concentrations of PpIX in meningioma cells after pre-treatment with 5-ALA in various concentra- tions (Tsai et al. 1999 ). To make matters even worse, PpIX is less phototoxic than the exogenous HPD or mTHPC, resulting in lower photodynamic effects at comparable concentrations and light doses (Madsen et al. 2002 ). Low PpIX concentra- tions and low phototoxic potential made suscep- tibility to PDT in 5-ALA pre-treated meningioma cells marginal (Tsai et al. 1999 ). Meningiomas were therefore not considered a suitable target for 5-ALA based PDT (Tsai et al. 1999 ). However, reports of consistent and frequent PpIX induced ﬂ uorescence in meningiomas after 5-ALA application encouraged further basic

documents.text: 40 pre-treatment of HBL-52 meningioma cells was related to a high ferrochelatase (FCH) activity. In comparison, PpIX synthesis was abundant in a benign meningioma (BEN-MEN-1) of low FCH activity. HBL-52 cells were irresponsive to PDT, whereas over 95% of the BEN-MEN-1 cells showed loss of vitality at light doses as low as 2 J/cm 2 (Hefti et al. 2011 ). Our ﬁ ndings show that FCH activity plays a crucial part in the accumula- tion of PpIX in meningioma cells, and that when the FCH activity is low, PDT for meningioma is feasible even under physiological conditions. This data further implies that FCH inhibiting substances could ensure sufﬁ ciently intense ﬂ uo- rescence after 5-ALA administration to allow local PDT of tumour remnants too risky to remove surgically, even in meningiomas nor-

documents.text: 5-ALA administration to allow local PDT of tumour remnants too risky to remove surgically, even in meningiomas nor- mally not prone to PpIX accumulation. Skull base meningiomas inﬁ ltrating the cav- ernous sinus, the orbit, the tentorial fold, Dorello’s canal or other anatomical regions where neurological functional integrity is at risk could be treated with PDT without the hazard of future dedifferentiation or carcinogenesis. 5-ALA based PDT could be a useful adjunct in the treatment of meningiomas, but as with PDD, the question whether heterogeneity in ﬂ uores- cence intensity and molecular heterogeneity cor- relate to some degree will be key to the usefulness of PDT in these challenging tumours. documents.text: angle (CPA). Their relationship to critical neural or vascular structures of the cpa is variable and they present with different signs and symptoms. The retromeatal located CPA meningiomas show a significantly larger tumour size and the diagnosis is made later compared to premeatally located CPA menin- giomas. Before the operation, a reduction of the facial nerve function and hearing function is present significantly more often in preme- atal CPA meningiomas. The clinical appear- ance of the retromeatal CPA meningiomas is dominated by cerebellar symptoms. Both pre- operative and postoperative impairment of facial nerve and auditory function prevailed in the premeatal group. These substantial differ- ences in relation to meningioma location within the CPA provide substantial evidence for the paradoxical observation that premeatal CPA meningiomas have a significantly worse postoperative functional outcome compared to retromeatal meningiomas although preme- atal meningiomas become already symptom- atic earlier and at smaller sizes. Introduction Generally, posterior fossa meningiomas have presented a formidable challenge throughout the history of neurosurgery. Some of these, espe- cially those arising from petroclival region, were considered “inoperable” up until the 1970s. However, with the advent of the newer modalities including operating microscope, computer tomo- gram (CT scan) and magnetic

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)

documents.text: with the advent of the newer modalities including operating microscope, computer tomo- gram (CT scan) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), microsurgical techniques as well as the development of various skull base approaches, it became possible now to resect the majority of posterior fossa meningiomas quite safely. Meningiomas compromise of approximately 12.5% of all intracranial neoplasms and occur at frequency of 5–8% in the cerebellopontine angle (CPA) (Langmann et al. 1990 ; Schaller et al. 1999a ; Schaller 2003a , b ; Spiriev et al. 2012 ). Next to vestibular schwannomas (VS), meningio- mas represent the second most common tumor in the CPA with a prevalence of 3–13% of all CPA lesions. The term CPA meningioma, although widely used, is somewhat imprecise in regard to the dural site of

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: of all CPA lesions. The term CPA meningioma, although widely used, is somewhat imprecise in regard to the dural site of attachment, because many of these tumors infact originate outside of the CPA. Meningiomas are believed to arise from cap cells of arachnoid villis, the site of cerebro- spinal ﬂ uid absorption which are distributed throughout the intracranial space, predominantly in relation to veins and dural sinuses. Meningiomas which involve the CPA may origi- nate from the posterior surface of the temporal bone adjacent to the internal auditory canal (IAC), from the meninges of the IAC itself, or from the inferior surface of the tentorium cere- bella. For this reason, their relationship to critical neural or vascular structures is quite variable, and they manifest with a wide range

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)

documents.text: their relationship to critical neural or vascular structures is quite variable, and they manifest with a wide range of signs and symptoms (Langmann et al. 1990 ; Schaller et al. 1999b ). With regard to clinical presentation and surgical management, CPA meningiomas may represent an even greater challenge than the more predictable vestibular schwannomas which always originate within the CPA. Therefore, we present modiﬁ ed classiﬁ cation of meningioma on the basis of above mentioned factors. A n a t o m y The posterior fossa, deeply concave, is formed by the occipital, the petrous and mastoid por- tions of the temporal bone. It is separated from the middle fossa in the median line by the basilar suture and on each side by the superior border of the petrous portion of the temporal bone. This

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)

documents.text: line by the basilar suture and on each side by the superior border of the petrous portion of the temporal bone. This serves for the attachment of the tentorium cere- belli, is grooved externally for the superior petrosal sinus, and at its inner extremity presents a notch, upon which rests the ﬁ fth nerve. Its cir- cumference is bounded posteriorly by the grooves for the lateral sinuses. In the centre of this fossa is the foramen magnum, bounded on either side by a rough tubercle, which gives attachment to the odontoid ligaments; and a little above these are seen the internal openings of the anterior condyloid foramina. In front of the fora- men magnum is the basilar process, grooved for the support of the medulla oblongata and pons Varolii, and articulating on each side with the petrous

documents.text: grooved for the support of the medulla oblongata and pons Varolii, and articulating on each side with the petrous portion of the temporal bone, forming the petro-occipital suture, the anterior half of which is grooved for the inferior petrosal sinus, the posterior half being encroached upon by the foramen lacerum posterius or jugular foramen. Above the jugular foramen is the IAC. Behind the foramen magnum are the inferior occipital fossae, which lodge the lateral lobes of the cere- bellum, separated from one another by the inter- nal occipital crest, which serves for the attachment of the falx cerebelli, and lodges the occipital sinuses. These fossae are surmounted, above, by the deep transverse grooves for the lodgment of the lateral sinuses. A. Belachew et al.

documents.text: 45 Pathology Meningioma arise from one of the layers of the meninges (Samii and Ammirati 1991 ; Samii and Draf 1989 ; Schaller 2005 ). These are usually benign, slow growing tumors and do not spread into normal brain tissue (Samii and Ammirati 1991 ; Samii and Draf 1989 ). A meningioma usu- ally grows inward causing pressure on the brain or spinal cord. It may grow outward toward the skull, causing the skull to thicken (Samii and Ammirati 1991 ; Samii and Draf 1989 ; Schaller 2005 ). Some of them may also contain cysts, small deposits of calcium or tightly packed bunches of blood ves- sels (Schaller et al. 1995 , 1999a ). History Harvey Cushing was the ﬁ rst to use the term “meningioma” in 1922, although this tumor was known under various names since the seventeenth century by French

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: term “meningioma” in 1922, although this tumor was known under various names since the seventeenth century by French surgeon Antoine Louis, who called it fungus durae matris (Kaye 2009 ). Various description and terms as well as site of origins followed thereafter (Bondy and Lee Ligon 1996 ; Okonkwo and Laws 2009 ). Meningiomas are now known to arise from arachnoidal cap cells of the leptomeninges. It was in 1915 as Harvey Cushing sided with Cleland and considered that meningeal tumors arose from arachnoidal cap cells. Seven years later, Harvey Cushing proposed the term “ meningioma” for these tumors and the term achieved global acceptance. Working together with his student, Percival Bailey, at the Peter Bent Brigham Hospital, he adopted a histopatho- logical classiﬁ cation system that

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: Percival Bailey, at the Peter Bent Brigham Hospital, he adopted a histopatho- logical classiﬁ cation system that listed four vari- ants: meningothelial, ﬁ broblastic, angioblastic and osteoblastic. Later, Bailey and Bucy expanded the initial histopathological classiﬁ ca- tion scheme of meningiomas in such a manner as the new WHO histological classiﬁ cation is almost the same despite new discoveries made by molec- ular biology, biochemistry and technology from the last years (Okonkwo and Laws 2009 ). Types and Classiﬁ cation The above mentioned WHO classiﬁ cation scheme recognizes currently 15 variations of meningiomas according to their cell type as seen under a microscope. These variations are called meningioma subtypes; the technical term for these cell variations is histological

documents.text: These variations are called meningioma subtypes; the technical term for these cell variations is histological subtypes. World Health Organization (WHO) meningioma classiﬁ cations WHO Grade I—Benign WHO Grade II—Atypical WHO Grade III—Malignant Meningiothelial Chordoid Papillary Fibrous (ﬁ broblastic) Clear cell Rhabdoid Transitional (mixed) Atypical Anaplastic Psammomatous Angiomatous Microcystic Secretory Lymphoplasmacyte-rich Metaplastic The vast majority of intracranial meningiomas (92%) have a benign histology, whereas remaining 8% show atypical or malignant features. For all meningiomas, the most common histopathological subtype is the meningotheliomatous type (63%), followed by transitional (19%), ﬁ brous (13%), and psamomatous (2%) meningiomas (Kondziolka et al. 1999a , b ).

documents.text: (63%), followed by transitional (19%), ﬁ brous (13%), and psamomatous (2%) meningiomas (Kondziolka et al. 1999a , b ). Fortunately, less than 10% of meningiomas are malignant (Park et al. 2009 ). In fact, meningiomas are the second most common tumor which arises in the CPA second most after vestibular schwannomas (Schaller and 5 Classiﬁ cation of Meningiomas in the Cerebellopontine Angle

documents.text: 46 Buchfelder 2006 ; Schaller et al. 2007 , 2008 ). CPA meningiomas arising from the dura of the posterior surface of the pyramid, lateral to the tri- geminal nerve, are deﬁ ned as CPA meningiomas (Gardner and Robertson 1988 ). In accordance with others, we also consider those meningiomas that arise from adjacent areas but have their main bulk in the CPA, as CPA meningiomas (Hitselberger and Gardner 1968 ; Schaller and Baumann 2003 ; Spiriev et al. 2011 ). Therefore, meningiomas constitute the second most frequent tumor of the CPA after the vestibular schwanno- mas and compromise 6–15% of all the CPA tumors (Hitselberger and Gardner 1968 ; Schaller and Baumann 2003 ; Spiriev et al. 2011 ). It is known that the origin of meningiomas is the arachnoid villi that are primarily found along

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: Spiriev et al. 2011 ). It is known that the origin of meningiomas is the arachnoid villi that are primarily found along major venous sinuses, especially around the superior sagittal sinus. Meticulous histological study has shown that these arachnoid villi can also be within the IAC and may serve as a site of origin for premeatal meningiomas. Nager and Masica ( 1970 ) found that arachnoid villi were distributed not only along the dural sinuses and in the gasserian envelopes but also along the greater superﬁ cial petrosal nerve, within the IAC, around the geniculate ganglion of the facial nerve, and within the jugular foramen. Guzowski et al. ( 1976 ) histologically examined 200 randomly selected temporal bones and conﬁ rmed the pres- ence of arachnoid granulations around the petrous apex,

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: 200 randomly selected temporal bones and conﬁ rmed the pres- ence of arachnoid granulations around the petrous apex, near the trigeminal impression, and in the sulcus for the greater superﬁ cial petrosal nerve. Although they could not ﬁ nd true arachnoid gran- ulations in the IAC, there were small clusters of arachnoid epithelium that could also serve as an origin of meningioma. The variable attachment sites and direction of the growth determine the high heterogeneity of CPA meningiomas and impose different clinical presentations as well as operative challenges. In relation to IAC, these can be broadly divided into two groups; premeatal and retromeatal meningio- mas (Gardner and Robertson 1988 ). This classiﬁ - cation has both the operative as well as the prognostic signiﬁ cance—the more

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)

metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: and Robertson 1988 ). This classiﬁ - cation has both the operative as well as the prognostic signiﬁ cance—the more medially located tumors are related to greater surgical complexity and to poorer outcome. CPA menin- giomas may develop therefore primarily anterior or posterior to the IAC and may extend either in a cranial or caudal direction (Samii and Ammirati 1991 ; Samii and Draf 1989 ; Schaller et al. 1995 ). Therefore, the orientation of CPA meningiomas to surrounding structures largely depends upon its site of origin (Jackler and Lalwani 1992 ; Samii and Draf 1989 ; Schaller et al. 1995 ). From a surgical standpoint, the pre- and retromeatal locations of CPA meningiomas correspond to the CPA cisterns, which have an anatomical distinc- tion in a superior and inferior part. The

documents.text: meningiomas correspond to the CPA cisterns, which have an anatomical distinc- tion in a superior and inferior part. The superior part of the CPA cisterns, in which premeatal meningiomas originate, contains the distal ante- rior inferior cerebellar artery (AICA), the laby- rinthine artery and the distal branches of the basilar artery (Spiriev et al. 2011 ). Retromeatal meningiomas, mainly located in the inferior part of the cerebellopontine cistern harbor the nerves of the jugular foramen (IX–XI) and the proximal posterior inferior cerebellar artery (PICA) (Spiriev et al. 2011 ). For this reason, there exist different clinical manifestations as well as opera- tive challenges. In addition, different types of intraoperative hemodynamic perturbations may be anticipated during resection of

documents.text: In addition, different types of intraoperative hemodynamic perturbations may be anticipated during resection of these meningiomas. Premeatal Tumor Location Increased frequency of tinnitus and vertigo her- ald a more intimate relationship between the tumor and the stato-acoustic complex as com- pared to CPA meningiomas posterior to the IAC. This may be explained because premeatal CPA-meningiomas compress, stretch and dis- place the facial-cochlear nerve complex and accompanying vascular structures posteriorly (Castellano and Ruggiero 1953 ; Langmann et al. 1990 ; Samii and Ammirati 1991 ). Sudden hear- ing loss, a common presenting symptom in ves- tibular schwannomas (Schaller et al. 1999b ), is rare in CPA meningiomas. This may be inﬂ u- enced by the different tumor histology with less

documents.text: et al. 1999b ), is rare in CPA meningiomas. This may be inﬂ u- enced by the different tumor histology with less intimate connection between the tumor and the neurovascular bundle in case of CPA- meningiomas particularly within the IAC. In regard to the risk of intra-operative hearing loss, it is generally accepted that loss of auditory A. Belachew et al.

documents.text: 47 function is more likely due to an interruption of the vascular supply of the cochlea rather than to mechanical factors like stretching or tension of the cochlear nerve. This vascular lesion is caused either by a vasospasm of the labyrinthine artery, induced by mechanical irritation of the neuro- vascular bundle or by direct injury and coagulation of labyrinthine vessels during dissec- tion of a more vascularized tumor. Therefore, it is not surprising that the postoperative facial nerve function as well as hearing is substantially worse in premeatal group of tumors in comparison to retromeatal tumors. This might simply reﬂ ect the increased risk of inadvertent surgical damage to the neurovascular bundle in anteriorly located tumors because the mostly posteriorly displaced stato-acoustic

documents.text: damage to the neurovascular bundle in anteriorly located tumors because the mostly posteriorly displaced stato-acoustic complex imposes a continuous obstacle during tumor removal. Retromeatal Tumor Location Retromeatal CPA meningiomas have a much more favourable outcome in comparison to ves- tibular schwannomas or premeatal CPA menin- giomas. Thus, they form a distinct clinical and surgical entity reﬂ ected by a number of speciﬁ c features. Decreased facial or cochlear nerve function is uncommon in these tumors. The signs of cerebellar compression, which are restricted to retromeatal CPA meningiomas, represent the main clinical characteristics. The oligosymp- tomatic presentation of retromeatal tumors may be explained by a larger cistern that accommo- dates tumor expansion (Samii and

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: presentation of retromeatal tumors may be explained by a larger cistern that accommo- dates tumor expansion (Samii and Ammirati 1991 ) and is reﬂ ected in the larger size of tumors becoming symptomatic. In general, retromeatal meningiomas are spared from the neurovascular complex of VII and VIII nerves by an intact arachnoid layer. They only displace, but do not inﬁ ltrate the facial-cochlear nerve complex. As described by us earlier (Schaller 2003a , b ; Schaller et al. 1999a ), the Vth cranial nerve was frequently displaced to the superior surface of the tumour, and the lower cranial nerves (IX–XI) were often distorted towards the foramen magnum. For these reasons, auditory and facial nerve function were found to be preserved post- operatively. Preoperative lower cranial nerves

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: auditory and facial nerve function were found to be preserved post- operatively. Preoperative lower cranial nerves dysfunctions may be anticipated in this group and its severity sometimes determine the extuba- tion of the patients postoperatively. In addition, impaired cough/gag reﬂ ex may impose greater chances of aspiration risks under general anesthesia. Risk Factors As noted earlier, meningiomas most often occur in people between the ages of 30 and 70 (Kaye 2009 ; Okonkwo and Laws 2009 ). Predilection for pediatric population is less likely. In compari- son to men, women are two times more prone for having meningioma (Kaye 2009 ; Okonkwo and Laws 2009 ; Schaller 2005 ) and they are also three times more likely to develop malignant meningioma (Schaller 2005 ). Spinal meningio- mas

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: 2005 ) and they are also three times more likely to develop malignant meningioma (Schaller 2005 ). Spinal meningio- mas occur 10 times more frequently in women than in men (Schaller 2005 ). Exposure to ionizing radiation, especially high doses, has been associated with a higher incidence of intracranial tumors, particularly, meningiomas. There is also evidence indicating a connection between meningiomas and low doses of radiation as well. The genetic disorder Neuroﬁ bromatosis type 2 (NF2) is other condition which usually associ- ated with higher risk of developing meningioma (Schaller 2003a , b ). Patients with NF2 also tend to develop more malignant or multiple meningio- mas (Schaller 2003a , b ). Per the Brain Science Foundation, a number of studies have suggested a correlation between

documents.text: mas (Schaller 2003a , b ). Per the Brain Science Foundation, a number of studies have suggested a correlation between meningiomas and hormones. Such ﬁ ndings include the following (Schaller et al. 1996 ):  Increased occurrence of meningioma in women  The detection of such hormones as estrogen, progesterone and androgen in some meningiomas  A link between breast cancer and meningioma  A connection between meningioma growth, menstrual cycles and pregnancy. 5 Classiﬁ cation of Meningiomas in the Cerebellopontine Angle

documents.text: 48 Symptoms Because meningiomas commonly are slow- growing tumors, they often do not cause notice- able symptoms until they are quite large. Some CPA meningiomas may remain asymptomatic for a patient’s lifetime or be detected incidentally when a patient has a neuroimaging for unrelated symptoms. Symptoms are typically present a mean of 2.5–5 years before the actual diagnosis (Schaller et al. 1995 , 1999a ). The presenting signs and symptoms depend on the size and loca- tion/extent of the tumor as described earlier here and can be related to brainstem compression, cer- ebellar compression, cranial nerve involvement as well as increased intracranial pressure caused by mass effect or hydrocephalus (Schaller et al. 1995 , 1999a ). Brainstem compression can result in long- tract signs, spastic

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: effect or hydrocephalus (Schaller et al. 1995 , 1999a ). Brainstem compression can result in long- tract signs, spastic weakness, and somatosen- sory changes in 15–50% of patients (Schaller et al. 1995 , 1999a ). Cerebellar compression can be related to dysmetria, ataxia, and unsteady gait and can be present in as many as 70% of patients (Samii and Draf 1989 ; Schaller et al. 1996 ). Headache and papilledema are signs of increased intracranial pressure and may be related to a large size of a tumor or to an obstruc- tion in the ﬂ ow of cerebrospinal ﬂ uid (CSF) that causes hydrocephalus (Samii and Draf 1989 ; Schaller and Buchfelder 2006 ; Schaller et al. 2007 ). It is difﬁ cult to differentiate small premeatal meningiomas from vestibular schwannomas pre- operatively (Schaller 2003a , b ).

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: cult to differentiate small premeatal meningiomas from vestibular schwannomas pre- operatively (Schaller 2003a , b ). The clinical symptoms caused by premeatal meningiomas are mostly identical to those caused by vestibular schwannomas and other lesions that occupy the IAC. Most of the cases initially manifest a hear- ing problem (Schaller et al. 1995 , 1999a ). The subtle difference is that facial nerve symptoms are more likely to occur with meningiomas than with vestibular schwannomas when the size is small (Schaller et al. 1995 , 1999a ). Needless to say, the facial nerve schwannoma should also be considered when the patient presents with facial nerve symptoms. Pre- und retromeatal meningiomas have typi- cal clinical development and symptomatiology (Schaller et al. 1995 , 1999a ).

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
