documents.text: und retromeatal meningiomas have typi- cal clinical development and symptomatiology (Schaller et al. 1995 , 1999a ). Premeatals tumors are diagnosed earlier and consequently are smaller. Their clinical presentation is with tri- geminal signs and facial and cochlear nerve signs, whereas retromeatal meningiomas present with cerebellar signs (Schaller et al. 1995 , 1999a ). Natural History Although most CPA meningiomas grow slowly and have a low mitotic rate, clinical beneﬁ t has been reported in many case series with either tumor regression or stasis after radiotherapy; however, these results have not been conﬁ rmed in randomized trials. Oya et al reported on the natural history of meningiomas (Oya et al. 2011 ). The prospect of benign meningioma growth is an important factor to consider in

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: of meningiomas (Oya et al. 2011 ). The prospect of benign meningioma growth is an important factor to consider in their proper management. Approximately 40% of 273 meningiomas (in 244 patients) grew within a 4-year period. Lack of calciﬁ cation, hyperinten- sity in T2 MRI, and peritumoral edema were pre- dictors of growth in follow up. In addition, age younger than 60 years and tumor size larger than 25 mm (diameter) were also associated with a greater risk. Neuroimaging Tumor features and extension can be best appre- ciated on the contrast-enhanced magnetic reso- nance imaging (MRI). On T1-weighted MRI studies, meningiomas are mostly isointense to slightly hypointense relative to brain parenchyma. On T2-weighted MRI studies, they show higher intensity than that of vestibular schwannomas.

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: to brain parenchyma. On T2-weighted MRI studies, they show higher intensity than that of vestibular schwannomas. Contrast enhancement is usually homogenous in both meningiomas as well as vestibular schwan- nomas. The radiological differential diagnosis between cerebellopontine angle meningiomas and vestibular schwannomas is based on several criteria. Usually, meningiomas are centered away A. Belachew et al.

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)

documents.text: 49 from the IAC and have broad contact with the petrous bone or the tentorium. The angle between the tumor and the pyramid is obtuse and the IAM is not widened. Although secondary invasion of the IAC might be observed in 10–22%, primary IAC meningiomas are very rare. Signal intensity of these masses on MRI will not contribute to the accurate radiographic diagnosis of the premeatal versus retromeatal meningioma. Both lesions are isointense to hypointense on T1-weighted MR images and are of variable signal intensity on T2-weighted MR images. They will also both brightly enhance after administration of contrast medium. Vestibular schwannomas that originate from the IAC comprise approximately 90% of CPA tumors (Brackmann and Bartels 1980 ). In this context, when a patient is found to have an

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: approximately 90% of CPA tumors (Brackmann and Bartels 1980 ). In this context, when a patient is found to have an enhancing mass in the IAC, it is usually assumed to be a vestibular schwannoma. Most of the reported cases of premeatal meningiomas were initially suspected to be vestibular schwannomas (Caylan et al. 2000 ; Haught et al. 1998 ; Hodgson and Kingsley 1995 ; Ishikawa et al. 1999 ; Langmann et al. 1990 ; Singh et al. 1975 ; Zeitouni et al. 1997 ). Nonetheless, there are some radiographic ﬁ nd- ings that should raise the suspicion of CPA menin- gioma. In vestibular schwannoma, the facial nerve usually lies anterosuperiorly to the tumor and is therefore encountered late in surgery (Schaller et al. 1999a ). This relationship is lost in CPA meningiomas, because the facial nerve may

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: late in surgery (Schaller et al. 1999a ). This relationship is lost in CPA meningiomas, because the facial nerve may lie along the posterior tumor edge and can be injured early in surgery (unless care is taken to identify it). Meningiomas in the IAC also have a tendency to involve adjacent nerve tissues or bones. Nager and Masica ( 1970 ) showed, by histological exam- ination, that meningiomas located in the IAC can invade the labyrinth and cochlea by following their individual nerve ﬁ bers to their ends. Meningiomas can also inﬁ ltrate widely into sur- rounding petrous bone marrow spaces and air cells. Conversely, dilatation of the IAC due to bone erosion is a more common radiologic ﬁ nd- ing with vestibular schwannomas and extensive bone invasion is unusual. Thus, the presence of bone

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: radiologic ﬁ nd- ing with vestibular schwannomas and extensive bone invasion is unusual. Thus, the presence of bone invasion around the IAC is suggestive the diagnosis of meningioma. Intraoperative MRI also is used during surgery to guide tissue biopsies and tumor removal. Magnetic resonance spectroscopy (MRS) is used to examine the tumor’s chemical proﬁ le and deter- mine the nature of the lesions seen on the MRI. Trigeminocardiac Reﬂ ex Signiﬁ cant decrease in heart rate (HR) and mean arterial blood pressure (MABP) occurs during tumor dissections near the trigeminal nerve at the brainstem (Arasho et al. 2013 ; Schaller 2004 ; Schaller et al. 1999b , 2009 ), also in CPA menin- giomas (Schaller et al. 1999b ). This phenomenon is called trigeminocardiac reﬂ ex (Arasho et al. 2013 ;

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: in CPA menin- giomas (Schaller et al. 1999b ). This phenomenon is called trigeminocardiac reﬂ ex (Arasho et al. 2013 ; Schaller 2004 ; Schaller et al. 1999b , 2009 ) and is described in more detail earlier in this book series (Arasho et al. 2013 ). Therefore all patients in whom the meningioma is removed in the CPA should undergo continuous monitoring of MABP and HR both during surgery and for at least 24 h after the procedure. It could be shown that preme- atal location as well as abrupt and sustained trac- tion on the trigeminal nerve is more prone to elicit the TCR in CPA than smooth and gentle traction (Sandu et al. 2015 ). A noninvasive, temporary pacemaker that can respond rapidly to abrupt changes in HR should be considered for patients with known cardiac risk factors and pre-

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: can respond rapidly to abrupt changes in HR should be considered for patients with known cardiac risk factors and pre- existing higher vagal tone to prevent the occurrence of the TCR (Arasho et al. 2013 ; Schaller 2004 ; Schaller et al. 2009 ; Sandu et al. 2015 ). Treatment Options Surgery CPA meningiomas primarily are benign tumors, frequently with deﬁ ned borders and often enabling complete surgical removal (Schaller 2003a , b ). The goal of surgery is to remove the CPA menin- gioma completely, including the ﬁ bers that attach it to the coverings of the brain and bone. However, complete removal can carry potential risks that may be signiﬁ cant, especially when the tumor has invaded brain tissue or surrounding veins. 5 Classiﬁ cation of Meningiomas in the Cerebellopontine Angle

documents.text: 50 Although the goal of surgery is to remove the tumor, the ﬁ rst priority is to preserve or improve the patient’s neurological functions. With patients for whom total removal of the tumor carries signiﬁ cant risk of morbidity (any side effect that can cause decreased quality of life as described ear- lier ), it may be better to leave some of the tumor in place and observe future growth with regular imag- ing studies (Schaller 2003a , b ). In such cases, the patient will be observed over a period of time with regular examinations and MRIs, while for other patients, radiation therapy may be deemed the best approach. It is common for patients to undergo pre- operative embolization of the tumor to ensure safety during the surgical procedure. Observation Observation over a period of time may

documents.text: of the tumor to ensure safety during the surgical procedure. Observation Observation over a period of time may be the appropriate course of action in patients who meet the following criteria (Schaller 2003a , b ):  Patients with few symptoms and little or no swelling in the adjacent brain areas.  Patients with mild or minimal symptoms who have a long history of tumors without much negative effect on their quality of life.  Older patients with very slow-progressing symptoms.  Patients for whom treatment carries a signiﬁ - cant risk.  Patients who choose not to have surgery after being offered alternate treatment options. Radiation Therapy Radiation therapy uses high-energy X-rays to kill cancer cells and abnormal brain cells, and to shrink tumors. Radiation therapy may be an option in

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: X-rays to kill cancer cells and abnormal brain cells, and to shrink tumors. Radiation therapy may be an option in the following cases (Kondziolka et al. 1999a , b ): Standard External Beam Radiotherapy uses a variety of radiation beams to create a conformal coverage of the tumor while limiting the dose to surrounding normal structures. The risk of long- term radiation injury with modern delivery methods is very low. Newer techniques of deliv- ery aside from 3-dimensional conformal radio- therapy (3DCRT) include intensity-modulated radiotherapy (IMRT). Proton Beam Treatment employs a speciﬁ c type of radiation in which protons, a form of radioactivity, are directed speciﬁ cally to the tumor. The advantage is that less tissue surround- ing the tumor incurs damage. Stereotactic Radiosurgery

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: cally to the tumor. The advantage is that less tissue surround- ing the tumor incurs damage. Stereotactic Radiosurgery (such as Gamma Knife, Novalis and Cyberknife) is a technique that focuses the radiation with many different beams on the target tissue. This treatment tends to incur less damage to tissues adjacent to the tumor. Currently, there is no data to suggest one delivery system is superior to another in terms of clinical outcome. Each has its advantages and disadvantages.  It is mainly used for small (<3 cm in diameter) residual or recurrent lesions when surgery is considered to carry a signiﬁ cantly high risk of morbidity.  It has been advocated as an effective management strategy for small menin- giomas and for meningiomas involv- ing the skull base or the cavernous sinus. 

documents.text: management strategy for small menin- giomas and for meningiomas involv- ing the skull base or the cavernous sinus.  It is used primarily to prevent tumor progression.  In a recently published series, the long-term follow up after radiosurgery was reported; a tumor control rate of 94% was found after an average of 103 months. documents.text: 51 Chemotherapy Chemotherapy is rarely used to treat CPA menin- gioma, except in atypical or malignant subtypes that cannot be adequately treated with surgery and/or radiation therapy. The current experience with chemotherapy is disappointing: Location in Relation to Surgical Treatment The two subtypes of location present with dis- tinct clinical features require different treatment strategy and have different prognoses. The more medially located the tumor, the greater is the sur- gical challenge and the poorer is the outcome. Conclusion The topological classiﬁ cation of CPA- meningiomas according to their location anterior or posterior to the ICA is important, because the clinical presentation, the surgical strategy to be applied, and the functional outcome of critical neural structures

documents.text: 62 Maintain Normocapnia .............................................. 62 Esmolol ...................................................................... 63 Indomethacin .............................................................. 63 Conclusion and Perspectives 63 References 63 Abstract There is considerable controversy and a surprising lack of data regarding cerebral hemodynamic status right after craniotomy. Cerebral hyperemia occurs frequently after brain tumor resection procedures. Therefore, it needs more clinical concerns. Initially, we start with reviewing studies focusing on postopera- tive cerebral hyperemia, outlining the changes of cerebral blood ﬂ ow and brain metabolism in the immediate postoperative period. Afterwards, we review the mechanisms currently thought to be

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: brain metabolism in the immediate postoperative period. Afterwards, we review the mechanisms currently thought to be responsible for postoperative cerebral hyper- emia. Impaired cerebral autoregulation, post- operative hypertension, hypercapnia and adaption to hypocapnia, hemodilution, the effect of anesthetics, sympathetic overactivity and surgery related factors are discussed respectively. Lastly, we introduce several avail- able monitoring techniques in the clinical set- ting, possible preventions and therapies of cerebral hyperemia after craniotomy. Further understanding of its pathophysiology will allow the rational development of prophylactic and therapeutical interventions of postoperative cerebral hyperemia, and eventually improve patient safety as well as outcome.

documents.text: 56 Introduction The brain is the organ that is most sensitive to isch- emia when blood supply becomes limited. On the other hand, the brain is also very vulnerable to excessive blood ﬂ ow. Postoperative cerebral hyperemia increases the risk of disastrous compli- cations, such as intracranial hypertension, cerebral edema, and intracerebral hemorrhage (Townsend and Knowles 1999 ; Noorani et al. 2010 ). Cerebral hyperemia is a complication which may occur after many medical proce- dures and situations, such as carotid endarter- ectomy (Noorani et al. 2010 ), removal of intracranial arteriovenous malformation (Chyatte 1997 ), or in the recovery period of cardiac arrest (Aaslid et al. 2003 ) and trau- matic brain injury (Macmillan and Andrews 2000 ; Khatri et al. 2012 ). It is known from

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: (Aaslid et al. 2003 ) and trau- matic brain injury (Macmillan and Andrews 2000 ; Khatri et al. 2012 ). It is known from peri-operative studies that cerebral hemodynamic change is a common phe- nomenon following complex intracranial surgery (Jabre et al. 1985 ; Asmussen et al. 1989 ; Engberg et al. 1989 ; Felding et al. 1994 ). But most of these early studies do not link cerebral blood ﬂ ow (CBF) with brain metabolism. Jabre et al. found the majority of the patients (75%) developed a signiﬁ cant rise in CBF in the immediate post- operative period (the ﬁ rst day after surgery), averaging 18% of the pre-operative value (Jabre et al. 1985 ). The limitations of Jabre et al.’s research are the comparatively small study popu- lation (19 patients with brain tumor) and the absence of brain

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: al.’s research are the comparatively small study popu- lation (19 patients with brain tumor) and the absence of brain metabolism status so that the cerebral hyperemia cannot be conﬁ rmed. On the other hand, Asmussen et al. detected a decrease of the arterio-venous oxygen content difference (A VDO 2 ) immediately after extubation, and the decrease sustained during the next 3 h (Asmussen et al. 1989 ). In their study, Asmussen et al. did not measure CBF at the same time. Neither did they measure the A VDO 2 before anesthesia. Recently, several researches have revealed that there is about 50% increase in the mean velocity of middle cerebral artery (Vmca) above the awake value at extubation, and the increase of Vmca is signiﬁ cant at least for 2 h after extu- bation in patients receiving

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: value at extubation, and the increase of Vmca is signiﬁ cant at least for 2 h after extu- bation in patients receiving craniotomy under general anesthesia. The simultaneous rise of the oxygen saturation of jugular venous bulb (SjvO 2 ) conﬁ rms the occurrence of postopera- tive cerebral hyperemia (Bruder et al. 2002 ; Grillo et al. 2003 ; Yang et al. 2013 ). Moreover, Yang et al. detected a persistent difference of Vmca between the tumor side and the normal side before and after craniotomy (Yang et al. 2013 ). Their ﬁ ndings indicate that the postop- erative cerebral hyperemia is much more pro- nounced on the side of the tumor. Pathophysiology At present, the mechanism of the postoperative cerebral hyperemic response is unclear. It is probably due to multiple factors involving brain

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: of the postoperative cerebral hyperemic response is unclear. It is probably due to multiple factors involving brain tumors, brain tumor resection surgery, and differ- ent functional and structural mechanisms of cere- bral blood vessels. Cerebral Autoregulation and Brain Tumor Cerebral autoregulation is the protective mecha- nism aiming to protect the brain from excessive changes in arterial blood pressure (Folino 2007 ). Autoregulation is a feature of many vascular beds, but is especially well developed in the brain, since it requires a high degree of homeostasis with regard to a balance of tissue nutrients and ﬂ uids (Townsend and Knowles 1999 ). This mechanism depends on the constriction or dilatation of arteri- oles in response to an increase or a decrease in arterial blood pressure

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: on the constriction or dilatation of arteri- oles in response to an increase or a decrease in arterial blood pressure (Rose and Mayer 2004 ; Kulic et al. 2008 ). Cerebral autoregulation has both myogenic and neurogenic components, the latter consisting predominantly of the sympathetic autonomic innervation (Townsend and Knowles 1999 ). Within the high and low autoregulatory limits of mean arterial pressure (MAP), normally ranging from about 50–150 mm Hg, CBF remains at a constant level of about 50 ml/min/100 g when at rest (Ravussin and Wilder-Smith 2001 ). Conventionally, it is considered that cerebral autoregulation comprised two main components: static and dynamic. Static autoregulation refers to CBF adjustments in response to steady changes X.-Y. Yang et al.

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: 57 in perfusion pressure. Dynamic autoregulation refers to the rapid regulation of CBF in response to changes in arterial blood pressure that occurs in a few seconds (Folino 2007 ; Ogoh 2008 ). These physiological mechanisms may be impaired by a variety of pathological entities, such as head injury, subarachnoid hemorrhage or hyper- tension, causing an increase in sensitivity to blood pressure ﬂ uctuation (Rose and Mayer 2004 ). The brain tumor vessels often lack the tight junctions that are normally present in cerebral microvessels, which results in dysfunction of the blood brain barrier (BBB). The low-grade astro- cytomas may contain vessels with close-to- normal barrier characteristics. In contrast, malignant primary tumors (i.e., high-grade astro- cytoma, glioblastoma) and brain

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: barrier characteristics. In contrast, malignant primary tumors (i.e., high-grade astro- cytoma, glioblastoma) and brain metastases of systemic cancers contain vessels that are exces- sively leaky and lack differentiated transport properties of normal BBB vessels (Lee et al. 2006 ). Up to two- thirds of intracranial meningio- mas are accompanied by peritumoral brain edema due to disruption of BBB with leakage of intra- vascular ﬂ uid and proteins out of vessels and into the brain’s extracellular space (Lobato et al. 1996 ; Nakano et al. 2002 ; Vignes et al. 2008 ). As a result, the presence of intracranial tumor can adversely affect cerebral autoregulation. Earlier researches reported the absence of cerebral auto- regulation in patients with intracranial tumors (Palvolgyi 1969 ; Paulson et

documents.text: reported the absence of cerebral auto- regulation in patients with intracranial tumors (Palvolgyi 1969 ; Paulson et al. 1972 ). These researches give rise to the concept of impaired cere- bral autoregulation in patients with brain tumors. When autoregulation is abolished, CBF becomes pressure passive, that is, it varies passively to ﬂ uc- tuations of systemic arterial pressure proportionally (Townsend and Knowles 1999 ). Thus the concept of impaired cerebral autoregulation further high- lights it as an important role in the pathophysiology of postoperative cerebral hyperemia. However, in the last decade, impairment of cere- bral autoregulation with brain tumor has been chal- lenged repeatedly. Growing evidence indicates that cerebral autoregulation is intact before and after tumor

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: been chal- lenged repeatedly. Growing evidence indicates that cerebral autoregulation is intact before and after tumor resection in most patients with brain tumors. Sharma et al. examined cerebral autoregulation and CO 2 reactivity in patients with supratentorial tumors undergoing elective tumor resection surgeries before and after surgery. They found that cerebral autoregulation was intact before and after tumor resection in 80% patients. Larger tumor size, and midline shift more than 5 mm were associated with impaired cerebral autoregulation (Sharma et al. 2010 ). Schmieder and colleagues tested dynamic cerebral autoregulation using transcranial Doppler (TCD) in patients with supratentorial tumors and found that autoregulation was preserved before and after tumor resection surgery

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: with supratentorial tumors and found that autoregulation was preserved before and after tumor resection surgery regardless of tumor location, size or histological classiﬁ cation of the lesion. The patients with accompanying diseases, such as diabetes mellitus and/or hypertension, how- ever, had signiﬁ cantly lower autoregulation values (Schmieder et al. 2000 ). The discrepancies in studies concerning cerebral autoregulation status could result from differences in characteristics of patients, brain tumors, or differences in methods of autoregu- lation testing. One thing to notice is that regional dysfunction of cerebral autoregulation may not be detected by devices which mea- sures global CBF. Postoperative Hypertension Postoperative hypertension is a signiﬁ cant con- tributor to

documents.text: which mea- sures global CBF. Postoperative Hypertension Postoperative hypertension is a signiﬁ cant con- tributor to postoperative cerebral hyperemia. Systemic hypertension is a common consequence of stress during neurosurgical recovery, due to sympathetic overdrive, with increased blood cat- echolamine concentrations leading to systemic hypertension and tachycardia (Bruder et al. 1999 ). The incidence of systemic hypertension after neurosurgery is more than 50% (Basali et al. 2000 ; Bilotta et al. 2008 ). Within the high and low autoregulatory limits of MAP and normal intracranial pressure (ICP), CBF remains con- stant. Exceed the upper limit of MAP, cerebral vessel constriction is insufﬁ cient to maintain cerebral perfusion pressure (CPP) at a constant level and the relationship of CPP

metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: is insufﬁ cient to maintain cerebral perfusion pressure (CPP) at a constant level and the relationship of CPP and CBF with MAP becomes linear, thereby cerebral hyper- emia is inevitable (Nemani and Manley 2004 ). In the presence of an impaired cerebral auto- regulation, CBF ﬂ uctuates even within normal range of MAP. Consequently, patients with dys- function of cerebral autoregulation are especially vulnerable to systemic MAP changes (Basali 6 Postoperative Cerebral Hyperemia—Pathophysiology and Clinical Concerns

documents.text: 58 et al. 2000 ). These observations have resulted in a great emphasis on aggressive control of blood pressure in the patient with brain tumor surgery (Fàbregas and Bruder 2007 ; Bilotta et al. 2008 ). Carbon Dioxide (CO 2 ) CO 2 is a potent cerebral vasodilator, resulting in a decrease in arteriole resistance and a correspond- ing increase in CBF (Curley et al. 2010 ). The effect of CO 2 on CBF depends on the individual patient, baseline blood ﬂ ow, and the central ner- vous system (Curley et al. 2010 ). Overall, for every 1 mmHg change in arterial blood pressure of CO 2 (PaCO 2 ), CBF changes by 1–2 ml/min per 100 g of tissue, when it is between 20 and 60 mmHg (Nemani and Manley 2004 ). Hypercarbia can cause severe cerebral hyperemia postoperatively. Increasing PaCO 2 to 79.5 mmHg will

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: and Manley 2004 ). Hypercarbia can cause severe cerebral hyperemia postoperatively. Increasing PaCO 2 to 79.5 mmHg will lead to an increase in CBF of greater than twice normal (Townsend and Knowles 1999 ). Impaired responsiveness to changes in PaCO 2 occurs in areas of vasoparalysis, which are associ- ated with extensive intracranial disease such as tumor, ischemia, trauma, stroke and infection (Townsend and Knowles 1999 ). At present, there are still much debates on whether vascular reactiv- ity to CO 2 remains intact in brain areas with tumor. Although Palvolgyi reported the loss of CO 2 reac- tivity in patients with brain tumors, Paulson et al. observed only a “dissociated vasoparalysis”, that is, loss of autoregulation with preserved CO 2 reac- tivity (Palvolgyi 1969 ; Paulson et al.

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)

metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: vasoparalysis”, that is, loss of autoregulation with preserved CO 2 reac- tivity (Palvolgyi 1969 ; Paulson et al. 1972 ). Dissociated vasoparalysis was described as a sign of tissue acidosis and was thought of as a stage in gradual transition from normal vasomotor func- tion to complete vasomotor paralysis (Paulson et al. 1972 ). Examined by TCD, Rao et al. found cerebrovascular response to hypocapnia was simi- lar between the normal and the abnormal cerebral hemispheres both under intravenous and inhala- tional anesthesia (Rao and Pillai 2006 ). Sharma et al.’s data indicate preservation of CO 2 reactivity in all patients with supratentorial tumors, and dis- sociated vasoparalysis occurring in 20% patients (Sharma et al. 2010 ). These studies suggest that vascular response to CO 2 is

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: vasoparalysis occurring in 20% patients (Sharma et al. 2010 ). These studies suggest that vascular response to CO 2 is mainly preserved in patients with brain tumor. But we should realize that all these clinical researches do not assess the cerebrovascular response to increased CO 2 ten- sions because of the risk of cerebral hyperemia and increasing intracranial pressure. Return to normocapnia after several hours of hyperventilation during anesthesia may also play a role in postoperative hyperemia. Contemporary use of hyperventilation has long been the mainstay of intraoperative management of intracranial hyper- tension during neuroanesthesia to facilitate access or to acutely reduce brain bulk (Gelb et al. 2008 ; Curley et al. 2010 ). It is an effective means of reducing elevated

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: or to acutely reduce brain bulk (Gelb et al. 2008 ; Curley et al. 2010 ). It is an effective means of reducing elevated intracranial pressure by inducing hypocapnic vasoconstriction of the cerebral arteri- oles, causing a reduction in CBF and a correspond- ing decrease in intracranial pressure (Curley et al. 2010 ). The effect of sustained hypocapnia on CBF decreases progressively because of buffering, which can normalize CBF as quickly as 6 h; subse- quent normocapnia can cause rebound cerebral hyperemia and increase intracranial pressure (Albrecht et al. 1987 ; Curley et al. 2010 ). However, several hours of hyperventilation does not cause postoperative cerebral hyperemia in patients with normal CO 2 activity undergoing abdominal surger- ies, indicating that adaption to hypocapnia may

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)

metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: in patients with normal CO 2 activity undergoing abdominal surger- ies, indicating that adaption to hypocapnia may not be a key factor among the mechanisms of postop- erative cerebral hyperemia (Yang et al. 2013 ). Hemodilution Most of the O 2 transported is reversibly bound to hemoglobin because the solubility of O 2 into blood is quite low. The amount of O 2 dissolved in the blood is thought to be negligible. Therefore, a decrease in the concentration of hemoglobin in the blood, as can occur through dilution after acute hemorrhage or acute ﬂ uid replacement therapy, results in a corresponding reduction in the O 2 -carrying capacity of the blood (Nemani and Manley 2004 ). With hemodilution, cerebral arterioles dilate and CBF increases to compen- sate for the reduced O 2 -carrying

documents.text: 2004 ). With hemodilution, cerebral arterioles dilate and CBF increases to compen- sate for the reduced O 2 -carrying capacity (Rebel et al. 2003 ; Nemani and Manley 2004 ). There is 2–3% increase in CBF for each percentage decrease in hematocrit (Bruder et al. 1998 ; Kulic et al. 2008 ). Whereas the balance between oxy- gen supply and demand is unchanged (Daif et al. X.-Y. Yang et al.

documents.text: 59 2012 ). Therefore, hemodilution does not cause absolute cerebral hyperemia. Anesthetic Agents It is generally accepted that some volatile agents can cause cerebral vasodilatation and contribute to cerebral hyperperfusion in a dose-dependent manner (Townsend and Knowles 1999 ; Randell and Niskanen 2006 ). But debates still exist in their effects on CBF at different concentrations. Autoregulation is not impaired with 1 minimal alveolar concentration (MAC) isoﬂ urane, but partially impaired with 1.5 MAC (Girard 2004 ). Sevoﬂ urane has been shown to be a less potent vasodilator than isoﬂ urane (Townsend and Knowles 1999 ). It is beneﬁ cial in preserving cerebral autoregulation. At least under clinical concentration (especially up to 1.5 MAC), it does not affect CBF (Bundgaard et al. 1998 ;

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: At least under clinical concentration (especially up to 1.5 MAC), it does not affect CBF (Bundgaard et al. 1998 ; Kaisti et al. 2003 ; Randell and Niskanen 2006 ). But some researches ﬁ nd that CBF changes are simi- lar between isoﬂ urane and sevoﬂ urane anesthesia at different MAC level in patients with brain tumor (Petersen et al. 2003 ; Fukui et al. 2010 ). Nitrous oxide (N 2 O), when addition of other anesthetics, has been reported to increase CBF, cerebral metabolic rate of O 2 (CMRO 2 ), and reduce cerebral autoregulation, but their magni- tudes are still being debated (Strebel et al. 1995 ; Iacopino et al. 2003 ). In contrast, decrease in CBF and CMRO 2 has been found during propofol anesthesia (Rasmussen et al. 2010 ; Klein et al. 2011 ). In a study of patients with brain tumor,

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: found during propofol anesthesia (Rasmussen et al. 2010 ; Klein et al. 2011 ). In a study of patients with brain tumor, the SjvO 2 was signiﬁ cantly higher and the A VDO 2 signiﬁ cantly lower during anesthesia with isoﬂ urane and sevo- ﬂ urane compared with propofol (Petersen et al. 2003 ). Cerebral autoregulation is preserved with propofol anesthesia (Harrison et al. 1999 ). In general, opioids have little to no effect on cerebral autoregulation or CO 2 reactivity. Coupling between CBF and metabolism is also maintained. Fentanyl does not affect CBF (Townsend and Knowles 1999 ). High doses of remifentanil decrease cerebral blood ﬂ ow veloc- ity despite perfusion pressure being constant in healthy male volunteers (Klimscha et al. 2003 ). Nevertheless, these doses are much higher than in

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: being constant in healthy male volunteers (Klimscha et al. 2003 ). Nevertheless, these doses are much higher than in the clinical setting. Likewise, modern nonde- polarising myorelaxants have minimal effects on intracerebral haemodynamics (Ravussin and Wilder-Smith 2001 ). It is also possible that prolonged anesthesia and residual effect of anesthetics might have affected postoperative CBF. However, most com- monly used anesthetics are short-acting and the blood concentration of them is quite low in the period of emergency from anesthesia. Recent studies of patients undergoing craniotomy make this assumption less plausible. In one study, Bruder et al. ﬁ nd no difference of postoperative cerebral hyperemia between propofol and isoﬂ u- rane anesthesia groups (Bruder et al. 2002 ). In

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: of postoperative cerebral hyperemia between propofol and isoﬂ u- rane anesthesia groups (Bruder et al. 2002 ). In another study from Yang et al., cerebral hyperemia does not occur in patients after abdominal surgery under isoﬂ urane anesthesia (Yang et al. 2013 ). Several studies ﬁ nd that postoperative cerebral hyperemia does not change proportionally with blood pressure or PaCO 2 . Furthermore, the CBF changes are more severely than those of blood pressure and PaCO 2 , indicating that some other factors may also contribute to its occurrence (Jabre et al. 1985 ; Asmussen et al. 1989 ; Bruder et al. 2002 ; Grillo et al. 2003 ; Yang et al. 2013 ). Sympathetic Overactivity It has been well established that many stressful events increase CBF and CMRO 2 (Fàbregas and Bruder 2007 ). Jabre et

documents.text: It has been well established that many stressful events increase CBF and CMRO 2 (Fàbregas and Bruder 2007 ). Jabre et al. ﬁ nd that the postopera- tive changes of CBF was not due to a difference in either PaCO 2 or BP. They believe that it reﬂ ects a normal reaction of the cerebral vasculature to the inevitable disturbance of surgery (Jabre et al. 1985 ). Grillo et al. further conﬁ rm the mecha- nism of the stress, which is associated with emer- gence and resultant sympathetic stimulation, since esmolol, a short-acting β-blocking drug, can blunt postoperative cerebral hyperemia (Grillo et al. 2003 ). However, esmolol decreases cardiac output, which may contribute to the decrease of CBF (Ogoh 2008 ). This mechanism has an assumption that cerebral BBB is disrupted and cerebral

documents.text: to the decrease of CBF (Ogoh 2008 ). This mechanism has an assumption that cerebral BBB is disrupted and cerebral autoregulation is abolished. Because intravenous infusions of norepinephrine has no 6 Postoperative Cerebral Hyperemia—Pathophysiology and Clinical Concerns

documents.text: 60 affected to CBF in both human (von Knobelsdorff et al. 1996 ) and animal studies (Myburgh et al. 1998 ). Besides, there are strong evidences indi- cating that increases in brain sympathetic activity could mediate vasoconstriction (Mitsis et al. 2009 ), which make this mechanism of postopera- tive sympathetic overactivity unsubstantiated. Surgery Related Factors The effect of neurosurgery on CBF remains sketchy and controversial. During tumor resec- tion, coagulate an artery or vein could possibly cause cerebral hyperemia. It is known that isch- aemia lasting as little as 30 s is sufﬁ cient to pro- voke a three- to four-fold increase in CBF. Such cerebral hyperperfusion has been documented in experimental animals during recovery from isch- aemia induced by temporary ligation of arteries.

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: has been documented in experimental animals during recovery from isch- aemia induced by temporary ligation of arteries. This hyperaemia affects the previously isch- aemic tissue only, and persists from minutes to hours (Pulsineli et al. 1982 ). Obstruction of venous drainage may lead to “occlusive hyper- emia”. In an animal experiment, occlusion of two cortical veins impaired cerebral autoregula- tion, that the cerebral autoregulation-curve lost its plateau-like appearance, with the propensity for hyperperfusion injury at a lower level of MAP (Schaller et al. 2002 ). Resection of brain tumor and the application of cerebral retractors may cause postoperative reactive cerebral hyperemia, which is similar as cerebral ischemia/reperfusion injury (Hai et al. 2004 ). Ischemia occurs under the

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: hyperemia, which is similar as cerebral ischemia/reperfusion injury (Hai et al. 2004 ). Ischemia occurs under the surgical brain retractors in 5–10% of patients (Ravussin and Wilder-Smith 2001 ). In the study of intracranial meningiomas and their surrounding tissue, Sergides et al. found that perilesional brain areas were ischemic (Sergides et al. 2009 ). Pennings et al. detected a decrease of oxygen pressure in peritumoral brain tissue, especially in patients with brain swelling, which indicating ischemia in peritumoral tissue due to increased local pressure. Furthermore, the oxygen pres- sure in peritumoral tissue increases after dura opening and tumor resection (Pennings et al. 2002 ). To protect the brain against ischemia, the microcirculation reacts to hypoxia through induction of

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: et al. 2002 ). To protect the brain against ischemia, the microcirculation reacts to hypoxia through induction of vasodilatation (Kulic et al. 2008 ; Ainslie and Ogoh 2010 ). After resection of brain tumor, the chronic ischemic brain area restores normal perfusion pressure. It is possible that autoregulatory vessels around the tumor might have disrupted BBB and impaired cerebral auto- regulation due to hypoxia (Ainslie and Ogoh 2010 ), therefore have been chronically dilated and become less responsive, their return to nor- mal caliber might occur gradually after tumor resection. In animal studies, autoregulation was found not to recover acutely after deﬂ ation of a balloon inserted into the brain to simulate a localized mass lesion (Miller et al. 1973 ). It can be regard as a kind of

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: inserted into the brain to simulate a localized mass lesion (Miller et al. 1973 ). It can be regard as a kind of reperfusion process. Interactions between reperfusion and the isch- emic tissue can lead to further tissue injury and disruption of BBB, which has been termed reperfusion injury (Khatri et al. 2012 ). Reperfusion injury occurring in chronic cere- bral hypoperfusion area is similar to acute cere- bral ischemia/reperfusion injury in rat model (Hai et al. 2004 ). This mechanism could involve in the development of postoperative cerebral hyperemia even in the presence of normal sys- temic arterial pressure. Clinical Complications Severe cerebral hyperemia, called ‘cerebral hyperperfusion syndrome’, can cause extended cerebral edema, intracranial pressure increase, intracerebral

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: ‘cerebral hyperperfusion syndrome’, can cause extended cerebral edema, intracranial pressure increase, intracerebral hemorrhage and death (Noorani et al. 2010 ). There is, however, limited reports of patients’ adverse events and outcome with less severe but acute, extensive, cerebral hemispheric postoperative hyperemia which is short-lasting and self-limiting. Postoperative cerebral hyper- emia may lead to devastating postoperative com- plications such as cerebral edema, intracranial hypertension, or cerebral hemorrhage (Townsend and Knowles 1999 ). Or maybe, it could poten- tially cause more insidious and subtle problems, such as memory loss, difﬁ culties with concentra- tion, and modiﬁ cation of thought processes (Falero et al. 2000 ). X.-Y. Yang et al.

documents.text: 61 In a retrospective case control study, researchers do ﬁ nd a link between perioperative hypertension and intracranial hemorrhage after craniotomy. Of particular interest was the very strong correlation between hypertension with intracranial hemorrhage when blood pressure remained in the normal range intraoperatively but became increased postoperatively. This suggests that loose surgical hemostasis per- formed at a low cerebral perfusion pressure may bleed when at a higher perfusion pressure (Basali et al. 2000 ). Moreover, the structurally weaker capillaries in brain tumor and peritumor region are more likely to rupture and bleed due to the distension during cerebral hyperemia (Sekhon et al. 1997 ). But a causal relationship between postoperative cerebral hemodynamic changes and the

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: hyperemia (Sekhon et al. 1997 ). But a causal relationship between postoperative cerebral hemodynamic changes and the risk of cerebral hemorrhage could not be conﬁ rmed yet. To present, we do not clearly know to what extent early postoperative neurosurgical compli- cations, such as cerebral hemorrhage or edema, may be related to postoperative cerebral hyper- emia. There is no deﬁ nite evidence from random- ized controlled trials to demonstrate its effect on patient’s outcome, either. Postoperative Brain Tissue Oxygenation/Metabolism Monitor Transcranial Doppler Transcranial Doppler (TCD) is a simple, reliable, non-invasive, bedside technique for beat-to-beat measurement of the CBF velocity in the basal cerebral arteries. TCD measures CBF velocity in the middle cerebral artery (MCA)

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: of the CBF velocity in the basal cerebral arteries. TCD measures CBF velocity in the middle cerebral artery (MCA) through a bony window in the temporal fossa (Aaslid et al. 1982 ). As the diameter of MCA is not affected by auto- regulation, TCD is a reliable index of assessing changes in CBF (Serrador et al. 2000 ). Many studies have conﬁ rmed a linear relationship between the changes of CBF velocity and that of CBF in most circumstances (Serrador et al. 2000 ; Rijbroek et al. 2009 ; Sorond et al. 2010 ). These researches validate TCD as a valuable tool to monitor CBF changes postoperatively. Measurement of CBF alone is insufﬁ cient because this parameter does not account for brain metabolism. However, several researches has shown a good correlation between Vmca and SjvO 2 in monitoring

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: for brain metabolism. However, several researches has shown a good correlation between Vmca and SjvO 2 in monitoring postoperative cerebral hyperemia (Bruder et al. 2002 ; Yang et al. 2013 ). Therefore TCD is a well-suited tech- nique in clinical setting for identifying postop- erative cerebral hyperemia. Monitoring the CBF velocity of tumor side is recommended, since the postoperative cerebral hyperemia is much more pronounced on the side of the tumor (Yang et al. 2013 ). Jugular Bulb Venous Oximetry Continuous or intermittent estimation of the global balance between cerebral oxygen demand and supply can be measured by jugular bulb venous oximetry. This is done by measuring the SjvO 2 through percutaneous retrograde cannula- tion of the internal jugular vein with a catheter with embedded

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: the SjvO 2 through percutaneous retrograde cannula- tion of the internal jugular vein with a catheter with embedded optical ﬁ bers (Nemani and Manley 2004 ). Normal SjvO 2 is assumed to be 54–75% (Macmillan and Andrews 2000 ). In the absence of anemia and changes in hemoglobin O 2 saturation, increase in SjvO 2 above 75% is indicative of absolute or relative hyperemia (i.e., supply in excess of metabolic require- ment). A value less than 50% reﬂ ects increased oxygen extraction and indicates a potential risk of ischemic injury (Macmillan and Andrews 2000 ). The major limitation of this technique is that it is unable to detect discrete regions of hyperemia or ischemia unless these are of sufﬁ - cient magnitude to inﬂ uence the saturation of brain efﬂ uent blood (Nemani and Manley 2004 ).

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: these are of sufﬁ - cient magnitude to inﬂ uence the saturation of brain efﬂ uent blood (Nemani and Manley 2004 ). Besides, its invasive nature also limits its use in postoperative settings. Near-Infrared Spectroscopy Near-infrared spectroscopy (NIRS) is a noninva- sive optical method for monitoring regional cerebral oxygenation by analyzing light absorp- tion of the brain. NIRS monitors mixed vascular 6 Postoperative Cerebral Hyperemia—Pathophysiology and Clinical Concerns

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: 62 O 2 saturation in capillaries, arteries, and veins within a small area beneath the sensor. NIRS may penetrate the cerebral cortex by only 2–3 mm, and therefore the region of cerebral tis- sues sampled probably extends no further than the gray matter (Folino 2007 ). Various studies have demonstrated that intraoperative transcra- nial regional cerebral oxygen saturation moni- toring using NIRS correlates closely with CBF, as conﬁ rmed by animal studies which show oxy- hemoglobin concentration is a reliable marker of perfusion (Hoshi et al. 2001 ). The major limitations of NIRS include inter- patient variability, variable optical path length, potential contamination from extracranial blood, and lack of deﬁ nable threshold (Folino 2007 ). Currently, it is considered a trend monitor, with

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: extracranial blood, and lack of deﬁ nable threshold (Folino 2007 ). Currently, it is considered a trend monitor, with each patient serving as control. In situations of potential regional hyperemia, bilateral monitor- ing should be used. Management of Postoperative Cerebral Hyperemia Blood Pressure Control Intensive systemic blood pressure monitoring and control has been recommended after crani- otomy (Hatashita et al. 1986 ; Mayhan 1990 ; Fàbregas and Bruder 2007 ). Postoperative poorly controlled hypertension coupled with impaired cerebral autoregulation, may increase the possi- bility of intracranial hemorrhage, most of which occurs within the ﬁ rst 6 h after surgery (Basali et al. 2000 ; Wong et al. 2012 ). The consensus is to control blood pressure at normal values, while the optimal

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: et al. 2000 ; Wong et al. 2012 ). The consensus is to control blood pressure at normal values, while the optimal blood pressure after craniotomy is still unknown (Fàbregas and Bruder 2007 ). Thus treatment of arterial hypertension should be based on physiological data of brain in the indi- vidual patient. Postoperatively monitoring tech- niques and equipment are advocated which allow the direct non-invasive monitor of brain, such as TCD and NIRS. Many drugs with different mechanisms can be used to control postoperative blood pressure after elective craniotomy. Esmolol, labetalol and metoprolol have been administered to blunt post- operative hemodynamic changes safely (Felding et al. 1994 ; Bilotta et al. 2008 ). Labetalol is given in small incremental doses of 0.25–1 mg/kg to a maximum

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: et al. 1994 ; Bilotta et al. 2008 ). Labetalol is given in small incremental doses of 0.25–1 mg/kg to a maximum dose of 2.5 mg/kg (Fàbregas and Bruder 2007 ). The short half-life of esmolol (9 min) allows a rapid titration of its effects in this context. Thus, esmolol seems particularly suitable to prevent the systemic and cerebral hemodynamic changes after neurosurgery. It is administered with a loading dose of 500 μg/kg followed by a continuous infusion rate of 100– 300 μg/kg/min (Bilotta et al. 2008 ). Administration of dexmedetomidine also has been reported to reduce the hypertensive response to emergence (Tanskanen et al. 2006 ). Calcium channel antagonists, nitroglycerin, and sodium nitroprusside are cerebral vasodilators, which may increase cerebral blood volume and ICP (Morimoto

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: and sodium nitroprusside are cerebral vasodilators, which may increase cerebral blood volume and ICP (Morimoto et al. 2000 ; Fàbregas and Bruder 2007 ). Moreover, nicardipine attenuates both static and dynamic cerebral autoregulation and increases CBF (Morimoto et al. 2000 ; Endoh et al. 2000 , 2002 ). Thus special cautious should be drawn when using these kinds of medicine to control postoperative hypertension after craniotomy. Nevertheless, blood pressure control alone is insufﬁ cient to avoid cerebral hyperemia after neurosurgery, due to the complexity of mecha- nisms which are involved in postoperative hyperemia. Maintain Normocapnia In the neurosurgical context, respiratory extu- bation criteria need to be strictly observed, such as adequate respiratory drive with acceptable

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: respiratory extu- bation criteria need to be strictly observed, such as adequate respiratory drive with acceptable oxygenation and normocapnia, state of con- sciousness, adequate muscle strength, and etc. (Ravussin and Wilder-Smith 2001 ). Low PaCO 2 during surgery should be gradually normalized as soon as is feasible (e.g. after adequate tumor debulking), which can decrease the possibility of rebound cerebral hyperemia and ICP rise, in sudden CO 2 normalization (Gelb et al. 2008 ; Curley et al. 2010 ). X.-Y. Yang et al.

documents.text: 63 Esmolol Esmolol is an ultra-short acting, highly cardio- selective β1- adrenergic receptor antagonist which is effective in treating arterial hyperten- sion and tachycardia in the perioperative setting (Bilotta et al. 2008 ). In a clinical study, esmolol alleviated postoperative cerebral hyperemia after craniotomy (Grillo et al. 2003 ). Cautious should be made as esmolol can also decrease cardiac output and blood pressure as well as heart rate (Grillo et al. 2003 ). Interestingly, esmolol does not affect CBF, cerebrovascular reactivity or cognitive performance in healthy volunteers or patients under anesthesia (Heinke et al. 2005 ; Saito et al. 2005 ; van der Starre et al. 2008 ). It is contraindicated in patients who are prone to bronchial asthma, have chronic obstruc- tive pulmonary

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: al. 2008 ). It is contraindicated in patients who are prone to bronchial asthma, have chronic obstruc- tive pulmonary disease, or a history of conges- tive heart failure. Indomethacin Indomethacin, a cyclooxygenase inhibitor, can act as a cerebral vasoconstrictor and decrease CBF. Animal experiments as well as clinical studies suggest the effectiveness of indometha- cin in the management of intracranial hyperten- sion and attenuates the cerebrovascular reaction to hypercapnia (Rasmussen 2005 ). Intravenous administration of indomethacin rapidly reduces CBF and ICP with no effect on CMRO 2 during craniotomy for supratentorial cerebral tumors (Bundgaard et al. 1996 ). The mechanism of CBF reduction by indomethacin is not fully understood. The clinical use of indomethacin in managing

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: ). The mechanism of CBF reduction by indomethacin is not fully understood. The clinical use of indomethacin in managing elevated ICP has been limited due to the risk of causing cerebral ischemia (Rasmussen 2005 ). However, the clinical reports of indomethacin are conﬁ ned to patients with severe head injury or during brain tumor resection. There has not been any clinical stud- ies regarding its effect in management of cere- bral hyperemia in the postoperative circumstances. Further studies are expected to elucidate the effect of postoperative use of indomethacin and its optimal dosage. Conclusion and Perspectives Clinical studies in patients with cerebral tumors provide evidence that postoperative cerebral hyperemia occurs frequently after brain tumor resection surgeries. The mecha- nism

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)

documents.text: evidence that postoperative cerebral hyperemia occurs frequently after brain tumor resection surgeries. The mecha- nism of postoperative cerebral hyperemia is probably multiple- factorial which includes dis- ruption of cerebral autoregulation, hyperten- sion and hypercapnia. Sympathetic overactivity under surgical stress and cerebral ischemia- reperfusion injuries may also involve. Postoperative cerebral hyperemia may lead to postoperative complications such as cerebral edema, intracranial hypertension, or cerebral hemorrhage. Blood pressure control and main- tain normocapnia can help to prevent postop- erative cerebral hyperemia. However, the reported clinical studies were performed mostly on inconclusive small patient materials using uncontrolled designs. Large-scale ran- domized

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)

documents.text: were performed mostly on inconclusive small patient materials using uncontrolled designs. Large-scale ran- domized studies are urgently needed to reveal detailed information and about cerebral hemo- dynamics in patients after brain tumor surger- ies. Further understanding of its pathophysiology will allow the rational devel- opment of prophylactic and therapeutical inter- ventions of postoperative cerebral hyperemia, and eventually improve patient safety as well as outcome. documents.text: 71 Management 72 Surgery ....................................................................... 72 Radiotherapy .............................................................. 73 Chemotherapy ............................................................ 73 Conclusion 74 References 75 Abstract Adenocarcinoma is the most common histologic subtype of lung cancer in most countries, accounting for almost half of all lung cancers. A considerable proportion of non-small-cell lung cancer [NSCLC] patients will develop central nervous system [CNS] metastases throughout the course of their dis- ease and these manifestations cause signiﬁ - cant morbidity and mortality. Thus, aggressive management of cerebral metastases is vital to preventing death from neurological causes and prolonging functional

documents.text: management of cerebral metastases is vital to preventing death from neurological causes and prolonging functional independence. This chapter focuses on current knowledge of pathology, imaging and management of brain metastases from lung adenocarcinoma and outlines a practical approach for the manage- ment of cerebral metastases. Introduction Cancer is the leading cause of death in economi- cally developed countries and the second leading cause of death in developing countries. About 12.7 million cancer cases and 7.6 million cancer deaths are estimated to have occurred in 2008. Lung cancer is the leading cancer site in males, comprising 17% of the total new cancer cases and 23% of the total cancer deaths. Among females, it was the fourth most commonly

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: 68 diagnosed cancer and the second leading cause of cancer death. Lung cancer accounts for 13% [1.6 million] of the total cases and 18% [1.4 mil- lion] of the deaths in 2008 (Jemal et al. 2011 ). Adenocarcinoma is the most common histologic subtype of lung cancer in most countries, accounting for almost half of all lung cancers. Brain metastases are the most common com- plication of systemic cancer and hospital and autopsy-based studies estimate that these tumors develop in 20–40% of all adult cancer patients (Posner and Chernik 1978 ). Empiric screening of patients with newly diagnosed NSCLC identify brain metastases in 3–10% of cases. Brain metas- tases, particularly those from NSCLC, constitute an increasing proportion of this global cancer bur- den. It is estimated that 40% of cancer

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: from NSCLC, constitute an increasing proportion of this global cancer bur- den. It is estimated that 40% of cancer patients will develop brain metastases and that 50% of all patients with brain metastases will have a primary lung malignancy (Yokoi et al. 1999 ). Taking everything into account, the presence of brain metastases is associated with high mor- bidity and mortality; in general, more than 50% of these patients will die within 6 months of diag- nosis. In the study of Nussbaum et al. ( 1996 ), the median duration from diagnosis of cancer to brain involvement was 3–6 months for lung can- cer and the duration from brain metastases to death was 4 months. The actual management of cerebral metasta- ses from lung adenocarcinoma, as with other metastatic tumors, is complex. This chapter

documents.text: management of cerebral metasta- ses from lung adenocarcinoma, as with other metastatic tumors, is complex. This chapter sum- marizes the crucial actions for the treatment of cerebral metastases from lung adenocarcinoma, also along with some common advices for other cerebral metastases. Pathology Brain metastasis formation in lung adenocarci- noma involves multi-step, highly discriminating molecular events with complex interactions between tumor and host cells. Following com- plex genetic alterations affecting tumor cell adhe- sion, motility, protease activity and internal signaling pathways, systemic tumor cells with a more motile and aggressive phenotype usually travel to the brain by hematogenous spread through the arterial circulation (Nathoo et al. 2005 ). All those complex steps have

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: the brain by hematogenous spread through the arterial circulation (Nathoo et al. 2005 ). All those complex steps have to be suc- cessfully completed for tumor cells to invade through blood–brain barrier [BBB], a dynamic interface that separates the brain from the circula- tory system, and survive. Seeding of tumor cells are more likely to be promoted with narrowing of cerebral vasculature and slowing of blood ﬂ ow and so brain metastases are commonly located beneath the gray-white matter junction and superﬁ cial distal arterial ﬁ elds. The distribution of brain lesions generally reﬂ ects the blood ﬂ ow. The most common locations for metastases are the frontal and temporal lobes, other lobes of the cerebrum, the cerebellum and diencephalic region. Unusual presentations were also seen

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: lobes, other lobes of the cerebrum, the cerebellum and diencephalic region. Unusual presentations were also seen (Samanci et al. 2011 ). After overcoming the BBB, tumor cells face up with components of the local microenvironment including the extracellu- lar matrix, resident brain parenchymal cells, and paracrine signaling molecules including cyto- kines and growth factors (Preusser et al. 2012 ). Once the metastatic cells have traveled to the ner- vous system, the brain provides adequate blood supply via angiogenesis, making brain metasta- ses grow and proliferate better. Although new knowledge is achieved over time, these mecha- nisms still remain unclear. Brain metastases usually form sharply demar- cated, spherical masses in the brain parenchyma. They often do not inﬁ ltrate the

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: usually form sharply demar- cated, spherical masses in the brain parenchyma. They often do not inﬁ ltrate the surrounding brain parenchyma, but cause edema due to mass effect. Larger tumors tend to have necrotic centers and the tumor is usually surrounded by an extensive amount of vasogenic edema. However, this edema is sometimes disproportional to the size of the lesions. Occasionally, metastases from lung carcinomas appear cystic. The lesions can be single or multiple. However, patients with lung adenocarcinoma are more likely to have multiple metastatic lesions. Nussbaum et al. ( 1996 ) exam- ined the records of 729 patients with brain metas- tases and 52% of the patients with lung cancer had multiple cranial lesions. The histological features of brain metastases from lung adenocar-

documents.text: with lung cancer had multiple cranial lesions. The histological features of brain metastases from lung adenocar- cinoma are usually similar to those of the primary neoplasm on microscopic examination (Fig. 7.1 ). M.Y. Samanci
