documents.text: 69 Neuroimaging Increased sensitivity and speciﬁ city of cerebral metastasis detection is possible with modern neu- roimaging techniques. Computed Tomography As Computed Tomography [CT] is a readily available and fast imaging modality, non-contrast CT scans are often the initial diagnostic test for symptomatic patients presenting to emergency department. On non-contrast CT scans, metasta- ses are typically hypodense to isodense, well- circumscribed solid lesions. Most metastases are surrounded by low attenuation vasogenic edema. Grey-white matter differentiation is maintained and the edema involves mainly white matter, extending in ﬁ nger-like fashion. The edema may be quite extensive in relation to the size of the lesion (Fig. 7.2 ). Asymptomatic patients with known systemic NSCLC are

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: quite extensive in relation to the size of the lesion (Fig. 7.2 ). Asymptomatic patients with known systemic NSCLC are screened for brain metastases with a contrast-enhanced study. Iodinated contrast enhancement is vital to the detection of metasta- ses on CT, and brain metastases may demonstrate ring, nodular, or solid enhancement. More metas- tases are visible on delayed imaging with high- dose contrast and the size of a given metastasis may appear to increase on delayed imaging (Sidhu et al. 2004 ). Computed Tomography may fail to identify lesions with small size and low contrast. Lesions missed on CT are almost always less than 10 mm in size. Although not as sensitive as magnetic resonance imaging [MRI], there is no clinical dif- ference between these imaging methods in terms of

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: as magnetic resonance imaging [MRI], there is no clinical dif- ference between these imaging methods in terms of survival time or rate in NSCLC. Alternatively, if contrast-enhanced CT shows multiple brain metastases, there may be little added value of obtaining brain MRI (Sofﬁ etti et al. 2006 ). Magnetic Resonance Imaging Magnetic resonance imaging is a sensitive screening test for brain metastasis and is sug- gested in cases where surgery or stereotactic radiosurgery [SRS] is planned, in order to detect additional lesions; in cases where CT is negative but there is a strong clinical suspicion for metas- tases in patients with known malignancy; and in patients for whom CT is not conclusive in deter- mining whether a lesion is neoplastic or non- neoplastic (Sofﬁ etti et al. 2006 ). On

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: CT is not conclusive in deter- mining whether a lesion is neoplastic or non- neoplastic (Sofﬁ etti et al. 2006 ). On MRI, most metastases are usually hypo- intense to iso-intense on T1-WI and iso-intense to hyper-intense on T2-WI. Surrounding edema is hypo-intense on T1-WI and more hyper-intense on T2-WI. documents.text: 70 adenocarcinomas on T2-WI has been documented in a few reports but no correlation was found regarding the presence of mucin, blood products, iron or calcium (Carrier et al. 1994 ). Small cortically based metastases may not demonstrate any visible edema, and can there- fore be missed on unenhanced MRI. Unenhanced MRI can also fail to demonstrate small nodules hidden by edema from other more dominant lesions. Gadolinium contrast enhancement is vital to detect small metastases and distinguish non-neoplastic white matter disease from metas- tases. But contrast enhancement may be altered by radiation, chemotherapy or steroid therapy. The standard gadolinium contrast dose is 0.1 mmol/kg of body weight and a useful tech- nique for increasing lesion detection is increas- ing the contrast dose

( b ) FLAIR image showing vasogenic edema extending to frontotemporoparietal and occipital white matter and marked midline shift. documents.text: 71 lesions greater than 10 mm will be seen at all dose levels, the utility of higher dose gadolinium mainly lies in the ability to reveal smaller lesions less than 5 mm. Studies utilizing incremental doses of intravenous contrast dose over the range of 0.05–0.3 mmol/kg resulted in more lesions detected (Baleriaux et al. 2002 ). Apart from increasing the contrast dose, delayed MRI with standard-dose gadolinium was found to be use- ful and one study concluded that the post-con- trast scan should be started with a 2–5 min delay after contrast medium administration (Akeson et al. 1997 ). Also some studies have suggested the use of standard gadolinium dose with mag- netization transfer, a technique that increases the contrast of enhancing and non-enhancing lesions by suppressing background

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)

documents.text: transfer, a technique that increases the contrast of enhancing and non-enhancing lesions by suppressing background signal, is equivalent to those reported for triple-dose gadolinium- enhanced MRI with conventional spin-echo techniques (Knauth et al. 1996 ). Higher ﬁ eld magnetic resonance magnets above 1.5 Tesla [T] now have widespread clinical availability and it has been shown that 3.0 T imaging increases subjective assessment of brain metastases (Ba-Ssalamah et al. 2003 ). Besides MRI tech- niques, contrast agents with higher T1 relativity have been shown to improve the sensitivity of brain metastasis detection (Colosimo et al. 2001 ). Metastatic tumors with higher tumor cellular- ity or high nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio can show restricted diffusion on diffusion-weighted images [DWI].

documents.text: cellular- ity or high nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio can show restricted diffusion on diffusion-weighted images [DWI]. Diffusion imaging aids in distinguishing metastases from pyogenic abscesses and can play a role in describing cystic from solid components in metastases (Bukte et al. 2005 ). A small study evaluating DWI signal intensity and apparent dif- fusion coefﬁ cient values of various histologic types of metastases found lower DWI signal intensity in enhancing portions of well- differentiated adenocarcinomas as compared with poorly differentiated carcinomas (Hayashida et al. 2006 ). Perfusion-Weighted Imaging can be used as an adjunct to conventional imaging to assess the degree of tumor microvascularity and to evaluate grading and malignancy. Perfusion imaging may help differentiate

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: the degree of tumor microvascularity and to evaluate grading and malignancy. Perfusion imaging may help differentiate between brain metastases and cerebral abscesses, which can appear identical on anatomic imaging (Hakyemez et al. 2006 ). Magnetic Resonance Spectroscopy provides semi-quantitative evaluation of metabolite levels to characterize brain tumors. Commonly mea- sured metabolites include choline, N-acetyl aspartate, lactate, lipids and creatine and creatine phosphate. N-acetyl aspartate is low or absent in metastases, consistent with the lack of neuroglial elements (Al-Okaili et al. 2006 ). Nuclear Medicine Techniques Single-photon Emission Computed Tomography [SPECT] and Positron Emission Tomography [PET] provide a means of examining regional cerebral blood ﬂ ow, metabolism, and

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)

documents.text: and Positron Emission Tomography [PET] provide a means of examining regional cerebral blood ﬂ ow, metabolism, and pharma- cology in vivo. These molecular imaging tech- niques rely on radiolabeled molecules and PET utilizes short-lived positron emitting isotopes, whereas SPECT uses lower energy γ-emitting isotopes. Positron Emission Tomography has the advan- tage that it is more sensitive, has higher intrinsic resolution and also allows for the evaluation of suspected metastatic lesions and whole-body staging in a convenient single examination. Single-photon Emission Computed Tomography is, however, cheaper and more widely available (Brooks 2005 ). Thallium-201 is a potassium analogue tracer for SPECT and has been used chieﬂ y in the eval- uation of primary tumors, but also shows accu-

documents.text: analogue tracer for SPECT and has been used chieﬂ y in the eval- uation of primary tumors, but also shows accu- mulation in metastatic tumors. One study reported a sensitivity of 70% for both thallium- 201 and Technetium-99 m-MIBI SPECT in the detection of cerebral metastases (Kojima et al. 1994 ). FDG-Positron Emission Tomography and PET are increasingly utilized tools particularly in the staging of lung cancer. FDG-Positron Emission Tomography has not been found to be as sensitive as MRI in the evaluation of brain metastases from lung cancer. Positron Emission Tomography was only 27% sensitive compared with contrast-enhanced MRI for brain metastases (Kruger et al. 2011 ). 7 Evidence-Based Approach for the Management of Cerebral Metastases from Lung Adenocarcinoma

documents.text: 72 Management Surgery There is now an important role for surgical resec- tion of metastatic brain tumors but a number of factors should be carefully considered for a patient with metastatic brain tumor in developing a treatment plan. The beneﬁ t of surgery in the treatment of brain metastases has been validated by data from randomized trials (Patchell et al. 1990 ; Vecht et al. 1993 ; V ogelbaum and Suh 2006 ). The study by Patchell et al. ( 1990 ) showed that surgical resection plus irradiation improved patient outcome in comparison to the irradiation alone. Median survival and functional indepen- dence was signiﬁ cantly longer in the surgical group. Vecht et al. ( 1993 ) randomized a group of 63 patients with solitary brain metastases and median survival was signiﬁ cantly longer in

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)

documents.text: 1993 ) randomized a group of 63 patients with solitary brain metastases and median survival was signiﬁ cantly longer in patients treated with surgery plus whole brain radiotherapy [WBRT] versus patients treated with WBRT alone. The Radiation Therapy Oncology Group [RTOG] used a recursive partitioning analysis [RPA] of patients with brain metastases to deﬁ ne three classes according to pre-treatment charac- teristics with prognostic signiﬁ cance to select homogeneously candidates for local interven- tions (Gaspar et al. 1997 ). Recursive partition- ing analysis class I patients have a Karnofsky performance score [KPS] ≥70 and age <65 years, with the primary tumor controlled and without extra cranial metastases. Recursive partitioning analysis class III is deﬁ ned by a KPS of <70, and RPA

documents.text: and without extra cranial metastases. Recursive partitioning analysis class III is deﬁ ned by a KPS of <70, and RPA class II covers the remain- ing patients. The median survival times for the 3 RPA classes are 7.1, 2.3, and 4.2 months, respectively. Patients in Class 1 are the best can- didates for aggressive treatment. Patients in Class 3 are unlikely to derive beneﬁ ts from inva- sive therapy. Patients in RPA Class 2 comprise a heterogeneous subset with intermediate survival rates, and for those who have a better prognostic index, such as controlled primary disease or good performance status, aggressive control of intracranial disease may still be justiﬁ ed (Pollock et al. 2003 ). In addition to considering these RPA class prognostic factors, it is also important to consider tumor

documents.text: et al. 2003 ). In addition to considering these RPA class prognostic factors, it is also important to consider tumor histology. Guo et al. ( 2012 ) assessed the impact of histologic subtypes on survival strati- ﬁ ed by the graded prognostic assessment [GPA]. The authors analyzed 780 patients with NSCLC brain metastases treated from 1982 to 2004. Graded prognostic assessment classiﬁ cation vari- ables included age, KPS, number of brain metas- tases, and presence of extra cranial disease. Mean survival time, in months, by histology was: ade- nocarcinoma 6.2 [n = 464], large cell 4.1 [n = 98] and squamous 4.2 [n = 108] [p = 0.0549]. For GPA 3.5–4.0, mean survival times did not differ signiﬁ - cantly by histology. Differences in mean survival times by histology were noted for GPA 3.0 [p =

documents.text: not differ signiﬁ - cantly by histology. Differences in mean survival times by histology were noted for GPA 3.0 [p = 0.04], GPA 1.5–2.5 [p = 0.01], and GPA 0–1.0 [p = 0.02]. For all patients with brain metastases, brain metastases from NSCLC, mean survival times by GPA score were: GPA 3.5–4.0, 12.6; GPA 3.0, 10.2; GPA 1.5–2.5, 5.8; and GPA 0–1.0, 2.7. Adenocarcinoma showed a statistically sig- niﬁ cant higher mean survival time than other his- tologies of NSCLC for patients with GPA 0–3.0. Patients with solitary brain metastases are the best candidates for surgical removal. But with better procedures, surgery is also an option for patients with multiple brain metastases. Bindal et al. ( 1993 ) reported similar survival rates for patients undergoing surgery for multiple metasta- ses to

documents.text: Bindal et al. ( 1993 ) reported similar survival rates for patients undergoing surgery for multiple metasta- ses to that of solitary metastases. The outcome of surgical resection for multiple metastases appears similar to that of patients having only single metastases for patients with high performance status and controlled systemic disease (Pollock et al. 2003 ). Surgery has been shown to improve survival and quality of life in patients with recurrent dis- ease. It provides an opportunity to conﬁ rm tumor histology; remove necrotic tissue; and use local- ized adjunctive therapies. Both radio-necrotic and recurrent tumors will beneﬁ t from surgical debulking and intraoperative histological conﬁ r- mation (Sills 2005 ). Recursive partitioning analysis provides a general framework for

documents.text: histological conﬁ r- mation (Sills 2005 ). Recursive partitioning analysis provides a general framework for patient selection, but in some occasions, surgical treatment may become an option for those who do not meet standard M.Y. Samanci

documents.text: 73 criteria, and vice versa. Surgery is the preferred treatment if there is a signiﬁ cant mass effect, if the therapeutic delay associated with radiation treatment is not acceptable and if SRS results in an unacceptably high radiation dose to the sur- rounding brain parenchyma. Although a small single lesion might be considered for SRS, exten- sive peritumoral edema, refractory seizures and resistance to radiation therapy lead one to sur- gery. Surgery might also be indicated for obtain- ing histology and sufﬁ cient tissue samples for providing optimal treatment (Sills 2005 ). Although with modern techniques, there are few areas of the brain that are considered surgically inaccessible, lesions that are deep and within elo- quent areas are inevitably associated with higher surgical

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)

documents.text: inaccessible, lesions that are deep and within elo- quent areas are inevitably associated with higher surgical morbidity. In metastases that impinge on eloquent areas or the motor strip, subtotal resec- tion may be the only option, especially if SRS is not appropriate (Tangjitgamol et al. 2004 ). Various advanced techniques and surgical adjuncts [stereotactic intra operative guidance, functional MRI, diffusion tractography, intra- operative MRI, awake craniotomy, intraoperative confocal microscopy] are in use for decreasing the risk of morbidity and enhancing the beneﬁ t of surgery (Sanai et al. 2011 ; Orringer et al. 2012 ). Taken together, these technologies enable more insightful operative decision-making and risk assessment. Radiotherapy Whole brain radiotherapy has been used as the

documents.text: insightful operative decision-making and risk assessment. Radiotherapy Whole brain radiotherapy has been used as the primary therapeutic modality for the treatment of brain metastasis. Median survival across all stud- ies based on previous RTOG protocols ranged from 2.4 to 4.8 months, thereby proving WBRT alone is inadequate in the long-term control of brain metastasis. After receiving WBRT, most patients experience neurocognitive sequel and declines in quality of life including headache, seizures, impaired mentation, cerebellar dysfunc- tion and motor deﬁ cits (Welzel et al. 2008 ). In patients with a solitary brain metastasis, random- ized studies have demonstrated improved overall survival when either surgery (Patchell et al. 1990 ) or SRS (Hatiboglu et al. 2013 ) is combined with

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)

documents.text: improved overall survival when either surgery (Patchell et al. 1990 ) or SRS (Hatiboglu et al. 2013 ) is combined with WBRT. Combination of WBRT with chemother- apeutics yielded disappointing results. Studies with Temozolomide in patients with brain metas- tases from NSCLC provided limited or no beneﬁ t compared with WBRT alone (Chua et al. 2010 ). However combining WBRT with targeted drugs resulted in decreased toxicities and increased median survival times. Welsh et al. ( 2013 ) reported acceptable toxicity proﬁ le and promis- ing median survival time in patients with brain metastases associated with NSCLC. But further studies with various chemotherapies and targeted therapies are needed. The beneﬁ t of prophylactic cranial irradiation [PCI] in small-cell lung cancer is well established

documents.text: are needed. The beneﬁ t of prophylactic cranial irradiation [PCI] in small-cell lung cancer is well established and it has also been investigated in patients with NSCLC, but with less compelling evidence of beneﬁ t. The authors concluded that PCI may reduce the incidence of brain metastases, but there is no evidence of a survival beneﬁ t and that there is insufﬁ cient evidence to support the use of PCI in clinical practice (Gore et al. 2011 ). To avoid toxicity issues of WBRT, SRS alone has been used in patients with better prognosis and a limited number of metastases. Pooled results from three randomized trials have revealed an apparent survival advantage in younger patients treated with SRS alone compared with WBRT and SRS. Although these studies demon- strated that patients with better

documents.text: treated with SRS alone compared with WBRT and SRS. Although these studies demon- strated that patients with better prognosis receiv- ing SRS alone had a similar survival to patients who received WBRT and SRS, a meta-analysis showed reduced local and distant control of brain metastasis after treatment with SRS alone com- pared with WBRT and SRS. But salvage with either repeat-SRS or WBRT was found to be comparable to initial treatment with WBRT and SRS (Tsao et al. 2012 ). There are also advancing radiotherapy techniques [‘under-dosing’ the bilateral hippocampi, hippocampal-avoidance WBRT] for possible use in the future. documents.text: 74 recent years. The upfront association of carboplatin plus pemetrexed was reported to allow simultaneous treatment of cerebral and systemic disease in patients with NSCLC with newly diagnosed brain metastases and appear to be particularly interesting in terms of radiologic response and overall survival (Bailon et al. 2012 ). Bearz et al. ( 2010 ) conﬁ rmed the good tolera- bility of Pemetrexed and reported a progressive disease in 12 patients [30.8%], a stable disease and partial response in 12 [30.8%] and 15 [38.4%] patients respectively, with an overall clinical beneﬁ t in 69% of patients. Single agent Temozolomide was reported to achieve a 41% disease control in patients with recurrent brain metastases from a variety of pri- mary malignancies, half of them suffering from NSCLC, with

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)

documents.text: with recurrent brain metastases from a variety of pri- mary malignancies, half of them suffering from NSCLC, with minimal toxicity (Abrey et al. 2001 ). In another study, authors concluded that there is a lack of therapeutic activity of single- agent Temozolomide in patients with stage IV NSCLC (Dziadziuszko et al. 2003 ). A study, which sought to conﬁ rm the beneﬁ t of adding Temozolomide to WBRT in patients with NSCLC with brain metastases, showed no signiﬁ cant dif- ference between the two treatment arms (Chua et al. 2010 ). Therefore, the role of Temozolomide in treating brain metastases remains unresolved. A randomized pilot trial investigated whether primary chemotherapy was feasible in terms of efﬁ cacy, survival, toxicity proﬁ le, and quality of life compared with WBRT given ﬁ rst

documents.text: was feasible in terms of efﬁ cacy, survival, toxicity proﬁ le, and quality of life compared with WBRT given ﬁ rst in chemotherapy- naive patients NSCLC with syn- chronous brain metastasis when neurologic symptoms or signs are absent or controlled by supportive care and it was reported that primary chemotherapy is more feasible and can be an appropriate option for patients with synchronous brain metastasis when neurologic symptoms or signs are absent or controlled (Lee et al. 2008 ). A phase III study to determine if the timing of WBRT with respect to chemotherapy with Cisplatin and Vinorelbine would inﬂ uence sur- vival in patients with NSCLC and concurrent brain metastasis suggests that the timing [early or delayed] of WBRT did not inﬂ uence survival of NSCLC with brain metastasis

documents.text: suggests that the timing [early or delayed] of WBRT did not inﬂ uence survival of NSCLC with brain metastasis treated with con- current chemotherapy (Robinet et al. 2001 ). Upfront systemic standard platinum-based combination chemotherapy rather than standard WBRT has been proposed as an alternative (Peters et al. 2012 ). The recent expansion of knowledge regarding the molecular biology of neoplasia and the meta- static phenotype has led to intense development of several targets of therapeutic intervention. Routine baseline tissue-based assessment of epi- dermal growth factor receptor [EGFR] mutations in patients with NSCLC and so treatment of these mutation-positive patients with EGFR tyrosine- kinase inhibitors [TKI] has been shown to result in improved survival (Rosell et al. 2012 ).

documents.text: with EGFR tyrosine- kinase inhibitors [TKI] has been shown to result in improved survival (Rosell et al. 2012 ). Epidermal growth factor receptor TKI therapy might be the treatment of choice for metastatic brain tumors in NSCLC patients harboring an activating EGFR mutation (Park et al. 2012 ). There is limited data on efﬁ cacy of anaplastic lymphoma kinase [ALK] inhibitor Crizotinib on brain metastases. In a study by Shaw et al. ( 2013 ), Crizotinib was found to be superior to standard chemotherapy in patients with previously treated, advanced NSCLC with ALK rearrangement. A meta-analysis demonstrated that Bevacizumab signiﬁ cantly prolonged overall sur- vival and progression free survival when added to ﬁ rst-line platinum-based chemotherapy in patients with advanced NSCLC (Soria et al.

Data on efﬁ cacy of other targeted thera- pies in lung cancer patients with brain metasta- ses, including BRAF inhibitors, HER2 inhibitors or RET inhibitors are lacking. Conclusion The following data could be used for basic man- agement of brain metastases along with some exceptions: • Computed tomography and MRI remain the primary modalities utilized for the detection of metastatic tumors of the CNS. • While certain ﬁ ndings on standard and advanced imaging are suggestive of a particular M.Y. documents.text: 75 diagnosis, imaging is not able to reliably pre- dict the histology of a brain metastasis. • For patients with single brain metastases, the data supports surgical resection for patients with good performance status and controlled primary cancer [RPA Class 1 and subset of Class 2]. • For patients with unfavorable prognosis [RPA Class 3], local aggressive treatment must still be considered for palliation of neurological symptoms or avoidance of death. • For patients with multiple brain metastases, the potential of a combined surgical/radiosur- gical approach holds promise. • For small [<2 cm], asymptomatic and deep- seated tumors, SRS provides a feasible non- invasive alternative. • Response rates of systemic therapy vary according to prior brain-directed treatments, number of previous

documents.text: ............................. 86 Motor Disorder ........................................................... 87 Cord Tethering ........................................................... 87 Discussion 87 Conclusions 92 References 93 Abstract We review a series of patients who underwent surgical removal of intramedullary spinal cord tumors, focusing on the long-term functional outcome, recurrence rates for the various tumors, and technical problems continually debated in neurosurgical practice. From December 1972 to December 2011, 202 patients underwent removal of intramedullary tumors. Lesions were located in the cervical spinal cord in 61 patients (30%), at a dorsal site in 60 (29%), at a cervicodorsal site in 51 (25%), and in the medullary cone in 30 (15%). The most frequent histological

The most frequent histological tumor types were astrocytomas (86 patients, 42%) and ependymomas (68 patients, 34%). Of the 68 ependymomas, 55 (81%) were completely removed and 13 (19%) incom- pletely removed. In 66% of the patients (42 patients), the presenting signs and symptoms remained unchanged at long-term follow-up; in 25% (16 patients), they improved; and in 9% (6 patients), the clinical status worsened. Of the 27 Grade I astrocytomas, 22 (81%) were completely removed and 5 (19%) incom- pletely removed. documents.text: 80 only 5 (12%) were completely removed, and 10% had improved. Only 2 of the 22 high- grade astrocytomas (10 Grade III astrocytomas and 12 glioblastomas) could be completely removed. The clinical postoperative status worsened in 14 patients (63.6%), was unchanged in 7 patients (31.8%), and there was 1 case of intraoperative death (4.5%). None of the 22 patients showed improvement in their neurological status postoperatively. In this series, excluding the 1 intraoperative death, all patients died of progression of the malignancy. Determinant predictors of a good outcome after surgery for intramedullary spinal cord tumors are histological type of lesion, complete removal of the lesion, and a satisfactory neurological status before surgery. Concerning the high grade astrocytoma, surgi- cal

documents.text: of the lesion, and a satisfactory neurological status before surgery. Concerning the high grade astrocytoma, surgi- cal treatment did not ameliorate the postopera- tive neurological status; instead, in the majority of cases, it prompted a worsening of the deﬁ cit. Radiotherapy and chemotherapy have a little inﬂ uence on the length of survival. In this series, multimodality treatment of intramedul- lary high-grade astrocytomas has been shown to increase length of survival without improv- ing the neurological status. Introduction Tumors of the intramedullary spinal cord are rel- atively rare lesions, accounting for only 2–4% of all central nervous system tumors (Stein and McCormick 1992 ). In adults, they account for approximately 20% of spinal tumors, whereas in children, the percentage

documents.text: McCormick 1992 ). In adults, they account for approximately 20% of spinal tumors, whereas in children, the percentage reaches 35%. The most frequent histological types of intramedullary tumors are glial tumors (80%), astrocytomas, and ependymomas, whereas oligodendrogliomas and subependymomas and gangliomas and caverno- mas are less frequent (McCormick and Stein 1990 ; Miller 2000 ; Schwartz and McCormick 2000 ). Hemangioblastomas account for 3–8% of all intramedullary tumors (Neumann et al. 1989 ) and in 15–25% are associated with von Hippel- Lindau syndrome, autosomal dominant inherited disease with incomplete penetrance (Parsa et al. 2000 ). Only 2% of all intramedullary tumors are metastases, usually from primary lesions in the lungs or breast (Raco et al. 1992 ). Ependymomas account

documents.text: tumors are metastases, usually from primary lesions in the lungs or breast (Raco et al. 1992 ). Ependymomas account for 15% of spinal tumors and, in adults, 60% of intramedullary spinal cord tumors, being the most frequent intramedullary tumor in adults. In patients older than 60 years, the incidence of astrocytomas and ependymomas is equal (Chang et al. 2002 ). The incidence of intramedullary tumors correlates closely with age. In children and young adults, astrocytomas, usually of low grade, are two to four times more frequent than ependymomas. Only 10% of astrocytomas in childhood are anaplastic lesions or glioblasto- mas, whereas in adults, the percent-age reaches 25% (Constantini et al. 2000 ; Epstein and Farmer 1990 ; Guidetti et al. 1981 ; Innocenzi et al. 1996 ). Astrocytomas in

documents.text: (Constantini et al. 2000 ; Epstein and Farmer 1990 ; Guidetti et al. 1981 ; Innocenzi et al. 1996 ). Astrocytomas in children show an indolent bio- logical behavior only in part explainable by the histological features, age being a favorable prog- nostic factor. Low-grade ﬁ brillary astrocytomas are biologically more benign in children than in adults. In a study conducted by Sandler, 60% of the patients younger than 21 years at diagnosis were still alive without recurrence 10 years after surgery, whereas for patients older than 21 years at diagnosis, the percentage dropped to 40% at 5 years without recurrence (Sandler et al. 1992 ). Among them, the late professor Beniamino Guidetti, Professor Gianpaolo Cantore, professor Roberto Delﬁ ni and myself A.R. Personal Series: Clinical Presentation and Surgery Patients Population A consecutive series of 241 patients underwent surgery for intramedullary tumors at the Neurosurgical Division of the University of Rome between December 1972 and December 2011. The population of 241 patients comprised 142 male patients (59%) and 99 female patients (41%), whose ages ranged from 8 to 72 years (mean, 42.3 years). A. Raco et al.

Histological features, site and extension were evaluated. Clinically, the duration of clinical symptoms before diagnosis and the presenting symptoms. Patients treated early in the series underwent diagnostic imaging with myelography or com- puted tomographic myelography or both proce- dures, and those treated from 1986 onward underwent gadolinium-enhanced spinal magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) (111 patients). Five patients with suspected vascular lesions also underwent spinal angiography. In all but 1 of 241 patients with intramedullary tumors treated, the tumors were surgically removed through a posterior median approach with a laminectomy, or a laminotomy in children, and a median continuous myelotomy or discon- tinuous myelotomy (20 patients) (Cantore et al. 2002 ). One patient harboring a ventrally placed intramedullary cavernous angioma was operated on via a transthoracic anterior approach. documents.text: 1990 onward, all patients underwent postoperative MRI follow-up within 72 h of sur- gery, at 4–6 months, and thereafter yearly. Of the 241 patients, 226 (94%) were available for “late” follow-up review. We analyzed our surgical and clinical results according to the various histological tumor types. Special attention was paid to III and IV grade astrocytoma according to WHO classiﬁ cation, that constitute a different subgroup. The average follow-up for the entire popula- tion of 190 patients available is 8.3 years. Progression-free survival (PFS), deﬁ ned as the absence of any clinical sign of recurrence of the disease, and overall survival were estimated by use of the Kaplan-Meier technique. The most frequent histological tumor types were astrocytomas (101 patients, 42%) and epen- dymomas, including one subependymoma (81 patients, 34%), followed by epidermoid- dermoid tumors (11 patients, 5%), hemangioblastomas (11 patients, 5%), oligodendrogliomas (5 patients, 2%), metastases (5 patients, 2%), neuromas (5 patients, 2%), and meningiomas (2 patients, 1%). The series also included 20 cavernous angiomas (8%), accounting for 22% of the 72 intramedullary neoplasms treated from 1991 to 2003. A speciﬁ c subgroup formed by 22 Grade III and IV astrocytomas were considered for the evaluation of results. The duration of clinical symptoms before diagnosis ranged from 2 months to 20 years (mean, 3.1 year), being shorter for group with malignant lesions (ranged from 2 months to 14 months, mean 6 months). The presenting symptoms and signs were hyp- esthesia or paresthesia or both complaints in 167 patients (69%), motor disorders in 48 patients (20%), and sphincter dysfunction in 26 patients (11%). The extension was, apart from two holocord tumors, from one to ﬁ ve vertebral segments. The neurological symptoms and signs at surgery included back pain in 72 patients (30%), radicular pain in 38 patients (16%), central pain in 50 patients (21%), paresthesias in 74 patients (31%), sensory deﬁ cits in 93 patients (39%), motor deﬁ cits in 132 patients (55%), and sphincter disturbance in 36 patients (15%). Personal Series: Operative Procedures Patients were placed in the prone position. For cer- vical and cervicodorsal lesions, a Mayﬁ eld skull clamp was used, and the head was ﬂ exed and ele- vated to reduce physiological cervical lordosis. Surgery began with a midline skin excision, followed by subperiosteal dissection of the mus- cle fascia. The lesion was then accessed with a laminectomy or, in children, a laminotomy. The spinal dura was incised at the midline and suspended away from the ﬁ eld to provide maxi- mum exposure. Fig. 8.1 ( a ) Graph showing Progression-free Survival (PFS) for

documents.text: 83 Under the operating microscope, the arachnoid membrane was incised above the midline: the arachnoidal layer was preserved as much as possible in an effort to limit postopera- tive scarring (Guidetti et al. 1981 ). In most patients, we preferred a longitudinal midline incision at the level of the posterior columns, or occasionally, a more lateral incision in the area of greatest cord swelling or bulging of the lesion. Once the tumor poles were identiﬁ ed, pial retraction sutures (5–0) were applied. These served to open the spinal cord wide without retracting it directly and to limit annoying cord pulsation under the operating microscope. In patients with glial lesions, intraoperative fro- zen sections were obtained for an intraoperative histological diagnosis to guide the surgical strat-

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)

documents.text: fro- zen sections were obtained for an intraoperative histological diagnosis to guide the surgical strat- egy. This was useful primarily in malignant tumors. If a cleavage plane was found, the tumor was ablated with a CUSA (Soma Technology, Inc., Bloomﬁ eld, CT) or with Sonopet (Stryker, Inc., Kalamazoo, MI) at low suction. If the neoplasm had inﬁ ltrated normal tissues, the Cavitron ultra- sonic surgical aspirator or the Sonopet was used to achieve internal decompression in the pulsed mode without suction, and for removing the areas close to the normal spinal cord parenchyma. In some patients, we elected to use a discon- tinuous myelotomy (Cantore et al. 2002 ) so as to avoid damaging the arteriovenous vessels on the dorsal surface of the spinal cord. For hemostasis, we prefer to use

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)

documents.text: to avoid damaging the arteriovenous vessels on the dorsal surface of the spinal cord. For hemostasis, we prefer to use Surgicel (Ethicon, Inc., Somerville, NJ) or ﬁ brillar Surgicel or irrigation, reducing as much as possible the use of bipolar coagulation. Once bleeding stopped, we removed the Surgicel to avoid artifacts in the postoperative MRI scan (Miyazawa et al. 2000 ). The dura mater was closed with a nylon monoﬁ lament running suture to pre- vent cord tethering, and a duraplasty was per- formed using Tutopatch, a dural substitute. Since the early 1990s, all patients have undergone peri- operative neurophysiological monitoring with somatosensory evoked potentials, and in the last 6 years, we used motor evoked potentials. Intraoperative ultrasound proved useful for localizing

documents.text: and in the last 6 years, we used motor evoked potentials. Intraoperative ultrasound proved useful for localizing nonbulging lesions, especially for small tumors such as cavernomas, and in more extensive masses for identifying the lesion poles. In the last ﬁ ve cases we used intraoperative MRI coupled with – in the last two cases – 1.5 T ﬁ ber tracting, to test the efﬁ cacy of such tech- niques in recognising tumor residual and intra- medullary long ﬁ bers involvement during surgical procedure. Personal Series: Surgical Results Independently of histopathological type, total removal of the tumor was signiﬁ cantly associated with a longer interval free of disease (Fig. 8.1b ). One patient (1%) died as a result of perioperative respiratory tract problems related to the disease. Mean follow-up was 7.1 years (range, 13 months to 20 year) in the 77 patients available (1 died, and 3 were lost to follow-up). PFS at 5 years was 83% (53 patients). In 51% of the patients (41 patients), the pre- senting signs and symptoms remained unchanged at long-term follow-up; in 38% (31 patients), they improved; and in 10% (8 patients), the clini- cal status worsened. In patients with ependymo- mas, the outcome depended strictly on the completeness of removal. In 7 of the 16 patients who underwent incomplete removal, tumor regrowth developed, causing neurological and clinical

documents.text: In 7 of the 16 patients who underwent incomplete removal, tumor regrowth developed, causing neurological and clinical evidence of compression. All 7 patients underwent reoperation, and 3 patients who had been treated early in the series and in whom we were not able to remove tumor at the second operation underwent radiation therapy at a mean dose of 30 Gy. Low Grade Astrocytomas Of the 101 astrocytomas, only 33 (31%) were completely removed. One patient who had a Grade I lesion died (1%), again as a result of peri- operative respiratory tract problems. 8 Intramedullary Spinal Cord Tumors: Long-Term Follow-Up After Surgery

documents.text: 84 The mean follow-up in the 81 patients avail- able (1 dead and 4 lost to follow-up) was 6.7 years (range, 5 months to 18 years). Of the 27 Grade I astrocytomas, 22 (78%) were completely removed and 7 (22%) incom- pletely removed. Functional assessment of the 28 patients available at “late” follow-up showed that 38% (11 of 28 patients) had improved, 8% (3 of 28 patients) had worsened, and 50% (14 of 28 patients) remained unchanged from preoperative status. One patient died and three patients were lost to follow-up. PFS at 5 years was 91% (21 patients) (Fig. 8.1a ). Conversely, of the 41 Grade II astrocytomas, only 9 (18%) were completely removed, and the remaining 41 (82%) were incompletely removed. One patient was lost to follow-up. Only 2 of the 22 high-grade astrocytomas could be treated with gross-total removal. Seventeen patients had a subtotal excision and 3 patients received only a biopsy with a duroplasty. documents.text: 86 Other Histological Types The 11 epidermoid-dermoid tumors were com- pletely removed in 45% of the patients (5 patients). The clinical symptoms remained sub- stantially unchanged from the preoperative status in seven of the eight patients who were available for long-term follow-up. In one patient, the clini- cal symptoms and signs progressively worsened, especially the motor symptoms, primarily owing to spinal arachnoiditis that developed in the long term. Two patients were lost to follow-up. Of the 11 hemangiomas, 10 (91%) were com- pletely removed. The patient who underwent incomplete removal was lost to follow-up. Of the remaining ten patients, seven are currently alive and three died as a result of causes unrelated to the intramedullary lesions. In patients with oligodendrogliomas,

In patients with oligodendrogliomas, com- plete removal was possible in 60% (three of ﬁ ve patients); two patients died 3 years after surgery, one died at 5 years, and the other two are still alive respectively 8 and 3 years after surgery. In all 5 patients with metastases, the lesions were radically removed. One patient was lost to follow- up; one died 3.5 years after surgery, and despite meticulous screening, the site of the primary lesion remained undisclosed; The three remaining patients died, one at 6 months, one at 16 months and the other at 18 months after surgery. The ﬁ ve patients who had schwannomas and the two patients with meningiomas all underwent complete removal of their tumors. In all seven

Personal Series: Clinical Long-Term Follow-Up Pain Most patients (153 of 190 patients, 80%) expe- rienced pain or painful paresthesias during the postoperative course. In most patients, these disturbances became gradually more bearable until in 30% of the patients (45 patients), they disappeared. In the 107 patients (56%) in whom pain persisted at follow-up, pain was equally distributed among the three characteristic types: spinal cord pain in 35% (37 patients), root pain in 40% (43 patients),

documents.text: among the three characteristic types: spinal cord pain in 35% (37 patients), root pain in 40% (43 patients), and sublesional pain in 25% (27 patients). Spinal cord pain is a burning, biting, unbearable sensation, a deep ache at the site of the surgical procedure. Sublesional pain is primarily a burning sensation often accompa- nied by paresthesia below the level of the lesion. In most of these patients, medical therapy satis- factorily relieved the symptoms (pain relief 50%). Gabapentin (Neurontin; Parke-Davis, Morris Plains, NJ) was used in an escalating manner, starting at 600 mg/day up to 1200 mg/ day for at least 4 months. Buprenorphine (Temgesic; Schering Plough, Kenilworth, NJ) was used as a second-line treatment (0.2 mg twice a day) in 15 patients. Of the 107 patients who had

documents.text: Kenilworth, NJ) was used as a second-line treatment (0.2 mg twice a day) in 15 patients. Of the 107 patients who had persistent postoperative pain, only 3 patients (2%) had chronic untreatable pain. All three patients had burning cord pain that was resistant even to intrathecal pump infusion of opioids (pain relief 50%). A common radiological ﬁ nding in these three patients was MRI evidence of dorsal cord tether- ing at the surgical site. In the one patient who agreed to undergo reoperation for this complica- tion, persistent postoperative pain remained unchanged. Dorsal tethering complicated 17% of the surgical interventions for intramedullary spi- nal cord tumors in this series. Of the patients classiﬁ ed preoperatively as McCormick Grade I, at 6 months after surgery, 65% remained func- tionally unchanged, 25% worsened to Grade II, and 10% worsened to Grade III. According to these results, the McCormick functional assessment, which was made during the follow-up neurological evaluation, shows 1 patient with McCormick Grade I, 5 with Grade II, and 15 with Grade III (there was 1 case of intraoperative death). documents.text: 17% of the cases (32 of 190 patients). In 8% (15 of 32 patients), tethering caused painful symp- toms. Reoperation for posterior cord tethering proved necessary in 5% of our series (9 of the 190 patients); in 4 of these 9 patients, the symp- toms subsequently regressed, and another 2 patients had a partial relief. The remaining 3 patients continued to complain of unbearable posterior column pain. A pump implanted for intrathecal opioid infusion failed to provide satis- factory pain relief. In the last 30 patients in the series, in whom we used a continuous dural monoﬁ lament nylon suture, only 1 patient had asymptomatic cord tethering. Discussion When our institute reported the long-term results for the treatment of intramedullary tumors in 1981, the operative technique was still

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: reported the long-term results for the treatment of intramedullary tumors in 1981, the operative technique was still controver- sial (Guidetti et al. 1981 ). Today, the surgical treatment of intramedullary tumors is well codi- ﬁ ed (Brotchi 2002 ; Jallo et al. 2001 ; Stein 1983 ). In the series of 202 patients we report here, who were treated in the microsurgical era, adju- vant treatment (chemotherapy and radiation ther- apy) had only a role in the management of high grade lesions, as it did in other reported series (Balmaceda 2000 ; Isaacson 2000 ; Minehan et al. 1995 ). Although the surgical management of intra- medullary lesions is now standardized, some technical reﬁ nements we routinely use make cer- tain problematic steps easier and avoid major pit- falls; these technical reﬁ

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: reﬁ nements we routinely use make cer- tain problematic steps easier and avoid major pit- falls; these technical reﬁ nements are of little use in malignant lesions, because the complete removal is often impossible. Pial retraction is an extremely helpful tactic that allows the neurosurgeon to divaricate the posterior columns in an anatomic and atraumatic manner; it also eliminates the annoying cerebro- spinal ﬂ uid pulsation that hinders surgical maneu- vers under the microscope. In our previously published series of intramedullary tumors, tumors had to be removed without the CUSA. The CUSA, now an indispensable tool in intramedul- lary surgery, especially the last-generation device with a microtip (Valleylab Division, Tyco Group LP, Boulder, CO), is used differently according to whether

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: device with a microtip (Valleylab Division, Tyco Group LP, Boulder, CO), is used differently according to whether it is needed for working within the lesion (pulsed mode, low suction) or for peeling the tumor bed (pulsed mode, no suction) (Guidetti et al. 1981 ). Coagulation must be reduced to a minimum, preferring hemostatic materials instead. Once bleeding has stopped, the operative ﬁ eld should be irrigated with physiological solu- tion to remove as much Surgicel as possible so as to avoid artifacts that may be misinterpreted at 8 Intramedullary Spinal Cord Tumors: Long-Term Follow-Up After Surgery

metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: 88 intraoperative or postoperative neuroradiological imaging. Another useful technical point is that except for laterally bulging lesions with paramedian extension documented on the preoperative MRI scan and seen even more clearly at surgery, we use a longitudinal midline medullary incision centered on the lesion. As a ﬁ rst step, we usually debulk the tumor, then we remove its lower and upper poles by angling the handle and the micro- tip of the CUSA. Conversely, if vessels of impor- tance cross the midline on the dorsal surface of the cord, we prefer to preserve them by means of a discontinuous myelotomy comprising two sep- arate incisions at midline level, one above and the other below the vessel or vessels involved (Cantore et al. 2002 ). We then remove the tumor with the CUSA at the

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: the other below the vessel or vessels involved (Cantore et al. 2002 ). We then remove the tumor with the CUSA at the level of the two incisions and later beneath the bridging area. The distance between the two incisions must be as small as possible to avoid excessive traction on the bridg- ing area. In the patients with intramedullary tumors in this series, these technical reﬁ nements proved especially useful in preserving spinal cord function. Even though Epstein and Farmer consider “extensive debulking using the CUSA critical before any effort is made to identify the interface between the tumor and adjacent spinal cord” (Epstein and Farmer 1990 ), we ﬁ nd that in remov- ing ependymomas, a cleavage plane must be sought immediately, preferably reducing the use of the CUSA to the minimum.

documents.text: ing ependymomas, a cleavage plane must be sought immediately, preferably reducing the use of the CUSA to the minimum. Since the early 1990s, all patients have under- gone perioperative neurophysiological monitor- ing with somatosensory evoked potentials. In the last 6 years, we routinely used motor evoked potentials. We currently use standard combined monitor- ing (SEPs and MEPs) in averaging modality with D-wave recording. We usually test patients before surgery because of the possibility that D-wave is not detected. Primarily Seps are very useful in the beginning of surgery to perform posterior myelot- omy when is difﬁ cult to identify the midline and anatomy is distorted by the tumor. Usually SEPs are lost during myelotomy and often they do not recover, but this is not a criteria to

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: by the tumor. Usually SEPs are lost during myelotomy and often they do not recover, but this is not a criteria to stop surgery because the SEPs loss do not correlate with a signiﬁ cant postoperative sensory or motor deﬁ cit. Secondarily, in debulking of the tumor, especially when margins are not so clear, MEPs and D-wave are very useful to predict a transient or a deﬁ ni- tive motor deﬁ cit. In fact, when MEPs are lost uni or bilaterally but D-wave is still unchanged or above 50%, we adopt some corrective measures: irrigation with warm physiological solution, cot- ton patties soaked in papaverin, increase of the average arterial pressure, modifying the site of removal or modify the removal technique (CUSA vs piecemeal removal using microscissors). Usually MEPs recover. If not, surgery can

metadata.creator: Adobe InDesign CS6 (Windows)
metadata.source: D:\New folder (3)\medical_agentic_rag\data\Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf
metadata.file_name: Tumors of the Central Nervous System, Volume 14_ Glioma, Meningioma, Neuroblastoma, and Spinal Tumors ( PDFDrive ).pdf

documents.text: modify the removal technique (CUSA vs piecemeal removal using microscissors). Usually MEPs recover. If not, surgery can pro- ceed and patient will suffer a transient motor deﬁ cit, which they usually recover in a short time. When MEPs are lost uni or bilaterally and D-wave decrease below 50%, surgery must be interrupted immediately. All the corrective proce- dure must be taken and if D-wave do not recover, surgery should be abandoned, otherwise patient will suffer a permanent motor deﬁ cit (Sala et al. 2006 ). Patient with deﬁ cit are monitored repeat- edly in the postoperative course in the following months to check any modiﬁ cations of the neuro- logical status. In my personal experience the D wave is the single most important factor in choosing wheth- ere to go or to stop the surgical

At the same time I do believe that increase of the blood pressure is the single most important factor in trying to balance a possible neurological deﬁ cit. Our experience shows that the surgical tech- nique must be planned according to the radio- logical appearance of the lesion. Neuroimaging techniques have had the same progress than sur- gical technique. All patients underwent neuroimaging using myelography and CT myelography before 1986, and from 1986 until the present, Gd-enhanced spinal MR imaging has been used. Currently, the introduction of MR imaging spec- troscopy for this type of disease allows us to acquire some data on the glial nature of the lesion and its mitotic activity, which aids our preopera- tive planning, that is really important especially for malignant lesions. Before the introduction of MR imaging spec- troscopy, our therapeutic protocol consisted of surgery, followed by adjuvant radiotherapy (Isaacson 2000 ) and chemotherapy (Balmaceda 2000 ) with TMZ. Nowadays, due to advance- ments in preoperative neuroimaging, our thera- peutic protocol consists of the following: (1) diagnostic suspicion based on clinical and radio- logical features; (2) biopsy; (3) histological con- ﬁ rmation;

documents.text: (1) diagnostic suspicion based on clinical and radio- logical features; (2) biopsy; (3) histological con- ﬁ rmation; (4) duraplasty; and (5) radiotherapy plus chemotherapy. The clinical pattern and the results of diagnos- tic neuroimaging frequently establish the diagno- sis, which should be conﬁ rmed by surgery or biopsy. This therapeutic strategy is mandatory in Italy, because the administration of medullary radiotherapy without histological conﬁ rmation is a medicolegal issue. Duraplasty is a fundamental step in the treatment of these lesions to prevent the complication of possible swelling of the spi- nal cord due to radiotherapy. Thus advanced neuroimaging techniques are fundamentals in diagnosis, in conditioning surgi- cal strategy and in early postoperative evaluation of surgical

documents.text: are fundamentals in diagnosis, in conditioning surgi- cal strategy and in early postoperative evaluation of surgical results. Although nowadays, the extent of surgical resection is based primarily on the early postop- erative MRI scan and secondarily on the neuro- surgeon’s impression, in the past, the only reliable means to judge the extent of removal was the sur- geon’s impression. Interestingly in the last few cases we used intraoperative MRI and ﬁ ber tracking. The utility of such techniques, differently from intracranial surgery, must be still evaluated. To date, we have never changed our surgical strategy according to the informations collected with intraoperative imaging techniques. Interestingly, diffusion tensor imaging trac- tography has been already used preoperatively to

documents.text: imaging techniques. Interestingly, diffusion tensor imaging trac- tography has been already used preoperatively to predict resectability of spinal cord tumors (Cantore et al. 2002 ; Setzer et al. 2010 ), but these reported preliminary data should be further conﬁ rmed. Further studies are needed to understand the usefulness of such imaging techniques, espe- cially in predicting short and long term clinical results. Our review underlines that the most important prognostic factor in determining postoperative neurological status in the long term is the patient’s functional status at surgery (Fig. 8.1c ), hence the need for an early diagnosis. An early diagnosis markedly inﬂ uences patients’ functional progno- sis and quality of life. It may be interesting to note that, because of the progress

It may be interesting to note that, because of the progress of neuroimag- ing, whereas in the ﬁ rst 10 years of our series, the average diagnosis time was 3.7 years, in the last 10 years, the duration of clinical symptoms before surgery was 2.1 years. In our institute all patients underwent pre- and postoperative functional assessment in which a simpliﬁ ed version of the McCormick classiﬁ ca- tion (Minehan et al. 1995 ) was used. Some patients were actually retrospectively classiﬁ ed according to the McCormick scale; this clinical evaluation seems to be very important for predic- tion of the functional postoperative status. This scale demonstrated an high prognostic value. In our series, even patients with large-sized tumors extending

In our series, even patients with large-sized tumors extending over several metameres that had not caused major functional motor deﬁ cits had a good chance of maintaining a stable neuro- logical status after surgical resection. In this series, two determinant predictors of long-term functional outcome and survival were the histo- logical type of tumor and the extent of surgical removal (Fig. 8.1a, b ). These two interrelated fac- tors cannot be analyzed separately. Accordingly, as others have reported, more than 80% of the Grade I ependymomas and astrocytomas in our series but only 10–15% of Grade II astrocytomas and none of the more aggressive glial tumors (anaplastic astrocytomas, oligodendrogliomas, and glioblastomas) could be

Some of the incomplete resection, espe- cially in ependymomas, were attributable to the fact that some remnants were visible only at post- operative MRI and were not detected at surgery; in four patients (high cervical lesion), resection could not be completed because of intraoperative vital function disturbance. Even in the patients with gross total resection, 20% of late deteriora- tion is expected, and it is a result of tumor recurrence. documents.text: Clearly, the poor prognosis of these highly aggressive tumors (Grade III astrocytomas and glioblastomas) in our series depended on both the histological grade and the incompleteness of sur- gical removal. The results of survival and PFS for ependymo- mas and astrocytomas in our series are deeply inﬂ uenced by the higher presence of astrocyto- mas rather than ependymomas (86 patients ver- sus 68). The comparison with other important studies shows remarkable differences. Constantini et al. 2000 , in their study of 164 pediatric patients with intramedullary tumors, reported a 3-, 5-, and 10-year PFS and survival, respectively, of 80, 71, and 54% and 80, 76, and 70% (Fig. 8.1d ). The 3-, 5-, and 10-year survival statistics in our series were 83, 73, and 42%; the 3-, 5-, and 10-year PFS

documents.text: 8.1d ). The 3-, 5-, and 10-year survival statistics in our series were 83, 73, and 42%; the 3-, 5-, and 10-year PFS statistics were 77, 70, and 34%. The main differ- ence between our series and that of Constantini regards the PFS and the survival at 10 years (PFS, 34 versus 54%; survival, 42 versus 70%). This difference may be explained by the fact that Constantini’s series is entirely a pediatric one, whereas our series is composed primarily of adult patients. Intramedullary astrocytomas account for ~1% of all CNS tumors and 6–8% of spinal cord tumors (Raco et al. 2005 ). The vast majority of intramedullary astrocytomas are slowgrowing lesions. Glioblastomas account for ~7.5% of all intramedullary gliomas and barely 1.5% of all spinal tumors (Miller 2000 ; Raco et al. 2005 ; Schwartz and

documents.text: ~7.5% of all intramedullary gliomas and barely 1.5% of all spinal tumors (Miller 2000 ; Raco et al. 2005 ; Schwartz and McCormick 2000 ). Unlike the intracranial variety, intramedullary high-grade astrocytomas have received little attention in the literature. This histotype shows a slight predilec- tion for patients in the earlier decades of life, (Constantini et al. 2000 ; Townsend et al. 2004 ) with the mean age ranging from 28.5 to 18.1 years according to the review of the literature by Fortuna and Giuffré in 1971 . In our series of 22 cases, the mean age (34.1 years) was higher; only 2 patients were younger than 15 years. According to the literature, the most affected spinal regions were the cervical and thoracic ones, especially the upper cervical region, (Guidetti et al. 1981 ;

As a rule, the clinical history is marked by initial symptoms that may be nonspeciﬁ c and are often underestimated (hypesthesia, paresthe- sia, and sensory deﬁ cits) (Ciappetta et al. 1991 ; Houten and Cooper 2000 ; Raco et al. 2005 ; Santi et al. 2003 ; Schwartz and McCormick 2000 ), whereas in the second stage, the neurological pat- tern worsens swiftly (pain, sensory and motor disorders, paraplegia, tetraplegia, and sphincter disturbances). documents.text: disturbances). In our series we found 3 cases of CNS dis- semination of the malignancy: 1 intramedullary and 2 intracerebral. In the literature several cases of CNS dissemination of intramedullary high- grade astrocytomas are described (Ng et al. 2001 ), but the mechanism of dissemination is still unclear; surgical manipulation may facilitate tumor cell seeding to the subarachnoid space, resulting with an intracranial dissemination. Immunosuppression is another mechanism that leads to CNS dissemination, but no patient in our series revealed this pathological mechanism in the routine serum examinations. As shown in Fig. 8.2c , the adjuvant treatment compared with surgery alone yielded an impor- tant improvement in survival (22 months com- pared with 10.6 months; p = 0.05, log-rank test). In our review we compared a group of 5 patients (4 with GBMs and 1 with astrocytoma) who were treated only with 40 Gy radiotherapy over the neuraxis with the group of 10 patients (4 with GBMs and 6 with astrocytomas) who were treated with radiotherapy and chemotherapy with TMZ. These data show an increase in mean survival time, although not a statistically signiﬁ cant one, of ~4 months (Fig. 8.2c ). We also compared survival with respect to the localization of lesions (Fig. 8.2d ), not taking into account the histological grade. Patients with tho- racic lesions fared much better than those with cervical lesions (22 months compared with 16.3 months; p = 0.3, log-rank test) even though, as expected, the numbers are too small to show a statistically signiﬁ cant increase in survival. None of the 22 patients showed any improve- ment in postoperative neurological status. The cause of postoperative worsening could be attrib- uted to increased spinal cord swelling due to edema or to surgical manipulation of the cord for an uncertain tumor margin. The prognosis for high-grade astrocytomas, like their intracranial counterparts, is extremely poor, and virtually all patients

As in patients with malignant intracranial astrocytomas, widespread leptomeningeal metas- tasis and hydrocephalus are common. The termi- nal event in many patients with to the cervicomedullary area, with respiratory paraly- sis. Pulmonary embolus and pneumonia may prove fatal to patients bedridden by progressive disease. Our experience in managing the cavernous angiomas in this series suggests that the ﬁ rst option for symptomatic lesions is total surgical excision to avoid recurrence and the risk of fur- ther hemorrhage. Especially in patients who pres- ent with mild paraparesis of relatively recent onset, the surgical outcome

Especially in patients who pres- ent with mild paraparesis of relatively recent onset, the surgical outcome combines low mor- tality with a high probability of functional recov- ery. When removing cavernous angiomas, we rarely ﬁ nd the CUSA or Sonopet helpful. For the surgical removal of hemangioblasto- mas, we use primarily bipolar coagulation to diminish blood ﬂ ow and shrink the lesion; this maneuver makes it easier to dissect the lesion away from the normal parenchyma, thus allowing en bloc removal. In excising dermoid and epider- moid lesions, the lesion must be meticulously dissected away from the spinal cord parenchyma with the help of microforceps and the dissector, removing the tumor piecemeal. In some cases, no attempt should be made to remove the tumor pseudocapsule

In some cases, no attempt should be made to remove the tumor pseudocapsule completely, because it often adheres tightly to the cord and its removal might provoke microhemorrhage and medullary petechiae. The surgical technique for removing intra- medullary metastases resembles that for glial lesions, even though the prognosis depends closely on the primary lesion. The small number of oligodendrogliomas in this series makes it dif- ﬁ cult to comment on the outcome of treatment. This ﬁ nding was also conﬁ rmed by the outcome in patients with cerebral ependy- mal lesions. From a technical viewpoint, removing an ependymoma is often an arduous task because these tumors tend more than other astrocytomas of similar grade to bleed intraoperatively. Nevertheless, because ependymomas usually have a better cleavage plane than low-grade astrocyte lesions, most of them can be completely removed. A special consideration should be made for our result according to the patient age. documents.text: tumors spanned a wide age range (8–72 years); 25 patients were children. Most tumors exhibited a more benign biological behav- ior in children than in adults. Despite the higher incidence of astrocytomas than ependymomas in children (18 astrocytomas versus 7 ependymo- mas), we found no signiﬁ cant difference in the prognostic criteria in the outcome in terms of quality of life and functional status between chil- dren and adults (Isaacson 2000 ). Thetering: postoperative spinal cord tethering is a problem frequently overlooked in most of the series regarding treatment of intramedullary tumors. In our experience, although 17% of the patients (32 of 190) had radiologically docu- mented cord tethering, only 8% (15 of 32 patients) were symptomatic. Drug-resistant postoperative pain is almost

documents.text: docu- mented cord tethering, only 8% (15 of 32 patients) were symptomatic. Drug-resistant postoperative pain is almost invariably associated with tethering of the spinal cord at the operation site. Dorsal tethering, in our experience, is not associated with deterioration of neurological function. Despite surgical revision and pain therapy, some patients with cord tethering remain untreat- able. Actually, as previously reported in the Results section, three of our patients who none- theless had a pump infusion device implanted had no relief. They should be considered as hav- ing a failure of the surgical therapy. Hence the importance of searching for the possible onset of cord tethering in the postoperative MRI scan so that debridement can be planned. Even though the literature documents

documents.text: cord tethering in the postoperative MRI scan so that debridement can be planned. Even though the literature documents complex techniques for reducing the incidence of cord tethering, we ﬁ nd that closing the dura mater with a running suture in monoﬁ lament nylon (5-0) instead of using silk interrupted stitches will sufﬁ ce (Goto et al. 2003 ). In managing large-sized tumors with solid and cystic components, we center the laminectomy or laminotomy on the solid component. If the solid component is removed, the coexisting syringo- myelic cavity disappears from the postoperative MRI scan within 2 months. The same surgical tactic provides a good outcome in cervical masses extending into the bulbocervical area. Conclusions From our experience in treating this large series of patients with

Conclusions From our experience in treating this large series of patients with intramedullary spinal cord tumors, we conclude that the determinant predictors of a good outcome after surgery for intramedullary spinal cord tumors are histological type of lesion, complete removal of the lesion, and a satisfactory neurological status before surgery. Surgery for intramedullary spinal cord tumors should aim at complete removal of lowgrade ependymomas and Grade I astrocytomas; “smart” removal of Grade II astrocytomas; and complete removal of cavernous angiomas, hemangioblas- tomas, and rare lesions such as schwannomas and intramedullary meningiomas. Radical surgery is neither advisable nor feasi- ble for epidermoid and other benign lesions in cases of dense

documents.text: Radical surgery is neither advisable nor feasi- ble for epidermoid and other benign lesions in cases of dense adhesion to the spinal cord, mak- ing removal a hard task that puts patients at risk of severe neurological deﬁ cits. Total surgical removal of the high grade lesions is rarely possible because of the anaplas- tic aspects; that is, inﬁ ltrative behavior. Surgical treatment did not ameliorate the postoperative prognosis prompting a worsening of the deﬁ cit. Radiotherapy and chemotherapy have a moderate impact on survival. Multimodality treatment of high-grade intramedullary astrocytomas has been shown, in our series, to increase survival without improving the patient’s neurological status. A. Raco et al.
